Please confirm or disconfirm this conjecture. If successful, discuss whether your proposed solution can already be found (a) nearly exactly in the literature, (b) whether a close version of it exists in the literature, or (c) if it is relatively novel. CONJECTURE_ID: 152
ARXIV_ID: 2602.21846v1
TITLE: Scalable Kernel-Based Distances for Statistical Inference and Integration
AUTHORS: Masha Naslidnyk
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.21846v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.21846v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.21846v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: main.tex:6088-6095

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Representing, comparing, and measuring the distance between probability distributions is a key task in computational statistics and machine learning. The choice of representation and the associated distance determine properties of the methods in which they are used: for example, certain distances can allow one to encode robustness or smoothness of the problem. Kernel methods offer flexible and rich Hilbert space representations of distributions that allow the modeller to enforce properties through the choice of kernel, and estimate associated distances at efficient nonparametric rates. In particular, the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD), a kernel-based distance constructed by comparing Hilbert space mean functions, has received significant attention due to its computational tractability and is favoured by practitioners. In this thesis, we conduct a thorough study of kernel-based distances with a focus on efficient computation, with core contributions in Chapters 3 to 6. Part I of the thesis is focused on the MMD, specifically on improved MMD estimation. In Chapter 3 we propose a theoretically sound, improved estimator for MMD in simulation-based inference. Then, in Chapter 4, we propose an MMD-based estimator for conditional expectations, a ubiquitous task in statistical computation. Closing Part I, in Chapter 5 we study the problem of calibration when MMD is applied to the task of integration. In Part II, motivated by the recent developments in kernel embeddings beyond the mean, we introduce a family of novel kernel-based discrepancies: kernel quantile discrepancies. These address some of the pitfalls of MMD, and are shown through both theoretical results and an empirical study to offer a competitive alternative to MMD and its fast approximations. We conclude with a discussion on broader lessons and future work emerging from the thesis.

Verdict

Disconfirm.

A concrete counterexample exists for p = 2. The stated assumptions do not force the projected one-dimensional laws u#P and u#Q to satisfy the J_p-integrability condition that the paper itself identifies as the real obstacle for p > 1. In fact, one can choose a rank-1 kernel so that e-KQD_2 becomes exactly the ordinary one-dimensional W_2 between empirical Gaussian laws, and then the claimed N^{-1/2} rate fails by a log-log factor. This is compatible with the paper’s own proof sketch, which says one “will need to show” E_{u∼γ} J_p(u#P) < ∞, but does not derive that from the conjecture’s hypotheses. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is not fully well-specified as written. The symbols γ and γ_L are not defined in the extracted statement, and the expectation is only over x_1,…,x_N,y_1,…,y_N even though the L^{-1/2} term comes from Monte Carlo sampling of projections u_1,…,u_L from γ. In the surrounding paper, γ_L is the empirical measure of L sampled directions, and γ is a measure on the RKHS unit sphere; that dependence is essential. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

More importantly, the paper’s own sketch for p > 1 says the key missing step is proving E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞, where J_p is the Bobkov–Ledoux quantity controlling one-dimensional empirical W_p rates. That condition is not implied by the conjecture’s stated assumptions (lower bounds on f_P,f_Q and a moment assumption on k(X,X)). So even before the counterexample, the hypotheses were already too weak for the proposed proof strategy. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Reasoning

Take
X = [0,1],
P = Q = Unif[0,1],
ν = Lebesgue measure on [0,1],
p = 2,
u(x) = Φ^{-1}(x),
where Φ is the standard normal CDF.

Now define the rank-1 kernel
k(x,y) = u(x)u(y).
Its RKHS is one-dimensional, spanned by u, and u has RKHS norm 1. Also
E_P[k(X,X)] = E[(Φ^{-1}(X))^2] = 1 < ∞,
so the conjecture’s moment assumption holds. Since P and Q are uniform on [0,1], their densities are bounded away from zero. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

Choose γ = δ_u. Then γ_L = γ for every L, so the Monte Carlo error is identically zero. Because the RKHS is one-dimensional and ν is Lebesgue, the definition of τ_2/e-KQD reduces exactly to the usual one-dimensional W_2 between the pushforward laws under u. Since u#P = u#Q = N(0,1), the population discrepancy is
e-KQD_2(P,Q;ν,γ) = W_2(N(0,1),N(0,1)) = 0,
while the empirical discrepancy is
e-KQD_2(P_N,Q_N;ν,γ) = W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N),
with \hat N_N,\hat N'_N the empirical laws of two independent N(0,1) samples. This reduction follows directly from the paper’s definitions of τ_p and e-KQD. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

So the conjecture would imply
E W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = O(N^{-1/2}).
I will show this is false.

Write Z_(j), Z'_(j) for the order statistics of the two independent Gaussian samples. Then
W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = (1/N) Σ_{j=1}^N (Z_(j)-Z'_(j))^2,
hence
E W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = (2/N) Σ_{j=1}^N Var(Z_(j)).
Now Z_(j) = q(U_(j)) with q = Φ^{-1} and U_(j) the jth uniform order statistic, U_(j) ~ Beta(j, N+1-j). For t_j = j/(N+1),
Var(U_(j)) = j(N+1-j)/((N+1)^2(N+2)) ≍ t_j(1-t_j)/N.
Using the mean value theorem and standard subgaussian concentration of order statistics around t_j, one gets
Var(Z_(j)) ≍ q'(t_j)^2 Var(U_(j))
for j away from the extreme edge; here q'(t)=1/φ(q(t)), with φ the standard normal density. Bobkov–Ledoux’s beta/order-statistics estimates are exactly the tools used in their one-dimensional W_p analysis. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

Near t = 0, Mills’ ratio gives
φ(q(t)) ≍ t sqrt(log(1/t)),
so
q'(t)^2 t(1-t)/N ≍ 1 / (N t log(1/t)).
Therefore
E W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N)
≳ (1/N) ∫_{1/N}^{1/2} dt / (t log(1/t))
≍ (log log N)/N.
Taking square roots,
E W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) ≳ sqrt((log log N)/N),
which is not O(N^{-1/2}). This contradicts the conjecture. The same tail mechanism is consistent with Bobkov–Ledoux’s Gaussian bounds and with Berthet–Fort’s exact Gaussian one-sample W_2 rate. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

So the exact conjecture fails already in this rank-1-kernel example. Conceptually, the failure occurs because the hypotheses allow a projection u whose pushforward law is Gaussian, and for p = 2 the Gaussian law has J_2 = ∞; Bobkov–Ledoux show that finiteness of J_p is exactly what governs the standard n^{-1/2} one-dimensional W_p rate. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

Literature overlap

The exact conjecture is already essentially in the literature: it appears almost verbatim as Conjecture 1 in Naslidnyk et al., “Kernel Quantile Embeddings and Associated Probability Metrics” (2025), Appendix C.5, with the same proof sketch based on reducing to Bobkov–Ledoux and then needing to prove E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞. So the conjectural statement itself is category (a): nearly exact in the literature. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

The disproof above is, as far as I could verify, not already written down in that exact kernelized form. But it is very close to existing literature: it is assembled from (i) the KQD definitions/reduction to one-dimensional projected Wasserstein, (ii) Bobkov–Ledoux’s necessary-and-sufficient J_p criterion for the standard n^{-1/2} one-dimensional W_p rate, and (iii) the known anomalous Gaussian W_2 rate. So the negative result is best classified as (b): a close version exists in the literature, while this exact counterexample seems to be a new synthesis rather than a verbatim published theorem. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

If one wanted a plausible corrected theorem, it would need at least an explicit assumption like
E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞ and E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#Q) < ∞,
plus a precise definition of the projection randomness in γ_L. That corrected form would be much closer to the Bobkov–Ledoux theory and to the proof sketch already in the paper. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Sources

Naslidnyk, “Scalable Kernel-Based Distances for Statistical Inference and Integration” (arXiv:2602.21846), thesis abstract page and the conjecture context in the PDF excerpt. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.21846))

Naslidnyk et al., “Kernel Quantile Embeddings and Associated Probability Metrics” (PMLR 2025 / PDF mirror), containing the same theorem for p=1, the definitions of τ_p and e-KQD, and essentially the same p>1 conjecture/proof sketch. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

Bobkov and Ledoux, “One-Dimensional Empirical Measures, Order Statistics, and Kantorovich Transport Distances,” especially Theorem 5.3 (upper bound via J_p), the necessity of J_p < ∞ for the standard rate, and Corollary 6.14 for Gaussian W_2. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

Berthet and Fort, “Exact rate of convergence of the mean Wasserstein distance between the empirical and true Gaussian distribution,” giving the exact Gaussian W_2 rate sqrt((log log n)/n). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.09817))

Rippl, Munk, and Sturm, “Limit laws of the empirical Wasserstein distance: Gaussian distributions,” for the general distinction between the null/same-distribution regime and the non-null/different-distribution regime in one-dimensional Wasserstein asymptotics. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/1507.04090))

"[1507.04090] Limit laws of the empirical Wasserstein distance: Gaussian distributions", "type": "url_citation", "url": "https://ar5iv.org/pdf/1507.04090"}], "logprobs": [], "text": "Verdict

Disconfirm.

A concrete counterexample exists for p = 2. The stated assumptions do not force the projected one-dimensional laws u#P and u#Q to satisfy the J_p-integrability condition that the paper itself identifies as the real obstacle for p > 1. In fact, one can choose a rank-1 kernel so that e-KQD_2 becomes exactly the ordinary one-dimensional W_2 between empirical Gaussian laws, and then the claimed N^{-1/2} rate fails by a log-log factor. This is compatible with the paper’s own proof sketch, which says one “will need to show” E_{u∼γ} J_p(u#P) < ∞, but does not derive that from the conjecture’s hypotheses. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is not fully well-specified as written. The symbols γ and γ_L are not defined in the extracted statement, and the expectation is only over x_1,…,x_N,y_1,…,y_N even though the L^{-1/2} term comes from Monte Carlo sampling of projections u_1,…,u_L from γ. In the surrounding paper, γ_L is the empirical measure of L sampled directions, and γ is a measure on the RKHS unit sphere; that dependence is essential. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

More importantly, the paper’s own sketch for p > 1 says the key missing step is proving E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞, where J_p is the Bobkov–Ledoux quantity controlling one-dimensional empirical W_p rates. That condition is not implied by the conjecture’s stated assumptions (lower bounds on f_P,f_Q and a moment assumption on k(X,X)). So even before the counterexample, the hypotheses were already too weak for the proposed proof strategy. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Reasoning

Take
X = [0,1],
P = Q = Unif[0,1],
ν = Lebesgue measure on [0,1],
p = 2,
u(x) = Φ^{-1}(x),
where Φ is the standard normal CDF.

Now define the rank-1 kernel
k(x,y) = u(x)u(y).
Its RKHS is one-dimensional, spanned by u, and u has RKHS norm 1. Also
E_P[k(X,X)] = E[(Φ^{-1}(X))^2] = 1 < ∞,
so the conjecture’s moment assumption holds. Since P and Q are uniform on [0,1], their densities are bounded away from zero. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

Choose γ = δ_u. Then γ_L = γ for every L, so the Monte Carlo error is identically zero. Because the RKHS is one-dimensional and ν is Lebesgue, the definition of τ_2/e-KQD reduces exactly to the usual one-dimensional W_2 between the pushforward laws under u. Since u#P = u#Q = N(0,1), the population discrepancy is
e-KQD_2(P,Q;ν,γ) = W_2(N(0,1),N(0,1)) = 0,
while the empirical discrepancy is
e-KQD_2(P_N,Q_N;ν,γ) = W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N),
with \hat N_N,\hat N'_N the empirical laws of two independent N(0,1) samples. This reduction follows directly from the paper’s definitions of τ_p and e-KQD. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

So the conjecture would imply
E W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = O(N^{-1/2}).
I will show this is false.

Write Z_(j), Z'_(j) for the order statistics of the two independent Gaussian samples. Then
W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = (1/N) Σ_{j=1}^N (Z_(j)-Z'_(j))^2,
hence
E W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) = (2/N) Σ_{j=1}^N Var(Z_(j)).
Now Z_(j) = q(U_(j)) with q = Φ^{-1} and U_(j) the jth uniform order statistic, U_(j) ~ Beta(j, N+1-j). For t_j = j/(N+1),
Var(U_(j)) = j(N+1-j)/((N+1)^2(N+2)) ≍ t_j(1-t_j)/N.
Using the mean value theorem and standard subgaussian concentration of order statistics around t_j, one gets
Var(Z_(j)) ≍ q'(t_j)^2 Var(U_(j))
for j away from the extreme edge; here q'(t)=1/φ(q(t)), with φ the standard normal density. Bobkov–Ledoux’s beta/order-statistics estimates are exactly the tools used in their one-dimensional W_p analysis. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

Near t = 0, Mills’ ratio gives
φ(q(t)) ≍ t sqrt(log(1/t)),
so
q'(t)^2 t(1-t)/N ≍ 1 / (N t log(1/t)).
Therefore
E W_2^2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N)
≳ (1/N) ∫_{1/N}^{1/2} dt / (t log(1/t))
≍ (log log N)/N.
Taking square roots,
E W_2(\hat N_N,\hat N'_N) ≳ sqrt((log log N)/N),
which is not O(N^{-1/2}). This contradicts the conjecture. The same tail mechanism is consistent with Bobkov–Ledoux’s Gaussian bounds and with Berthet–Fort’s exact Gaussian one-sample W_2 rate. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

So the exact conjecture fails already in this rank-1-kernel example. Conceptually, the failure occurs because the hypotheses allow a projection u whose pushforward law is Gaussian, and for p = 2 the Gaussian law has J_2 = ∞; Bobkov–Ledoux show that finiteness of J_p is exactly what governs the standard n^{-1/2} one-dimensional W_p rate. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

Literature overlap

The exact conjecture is already essentially in the literature: it appears almost verbatim as Conjecture 1 in Naslidnyk et al., “Kernel Quantile Embeddings and Associated Probability Metrics” (2025), Appendix C.5, with the same proof sketch based on reducing to Bobkov–Ledoux and then needing to prove E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞. So the conjectural statement itself is category (a): nearly exact in the literature. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

The disproof above is, as far as I could verify, not already written down in that exact kernelized form. But it is very close to existing literature: it is assembled from (i) the KQD definitions/reduction to one-dimensional projected Wasserstein, (ii) Bobkov–Ledoux’s necessary-and-sufficient J_p criterion for the standard n^{-1/2} one-dimensional W_p rate, and (iii) the known anomalous Gaussian W_2 rate. So the negative result is best classified as (b): a close version exists in the literature, while this exact counterexample seems to be a new synthesis rather than a verbatim published theorem. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

If one wanted a plausible corrected theorem, it would need at least an explicit assumption like
E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#P) < ∞ and E_{u∼γ}J_p(u#Q) < ∞,
plus a precise definition of the projection randomness in γ_L. That corrected form would be much closer to the Bobkov–Ledoux theory and to the proof sketch already in the paper. ([discovery.ucl.ac.uk](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10211533/1/2505.20433v1.pdf))

Sources

Naslidnyk, “Scalable Kernel-Based Distances for Statistical Inference and Integration” (arXiv:2602.21846), thesis abstract page and the conjecture context in the PDF excerpt. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.21846))

Naslidnyk et al., “Kernel Quantile Embeddings and Associated Probability Metrics” (PMLR 2025 / PDF mirror), containing the same theorem for p=1, the definitions of τ_p and e-KQD, and essentially the same p>1 conjecture/proof sketch. ([mashanaslidnyk.github.io](https://mashanaslidnyk.github.io/assets/pdf/kqe.pdf))

Bobkov and Ledoux, “One-Dimensional Empirical Measures, Order Statistics, and Kantorovich Transport Distances,” especially Theorem 5.3 (upper bound via J_p), the necessity of J_p < ∞ for the standard rate, and Corollary 6.14 for Gaussian W_2. ([www-users.cse.umn.edu](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~bobko001/preprints/2016_BL_Order.statistics_Revised.version.pdf))

CONJECTURE_ID: 368
ARXIV_ID: 2602.13960v1
TITLE: Steady-State Behavior of Constant-Stepsize Stochastic Approximation: Gaussian Approximation and Tail Bounds
AUTHORS: Zedong Wang, Yuyang Wang, Ijay Narang, Felix Wang, Yuzhou Wang, Siva Theja Maguluri
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13960v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.13960v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: Arxiv_Version.tex:797-804

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Constant-stepsize stochastic approximation (SA) is widely used in learning for computational efficiency. For a fixed stepsize, the iterates typically admit a stationary distribution that is rarely tractable. Prior work shows that as the stepsize $α\downarrow 0$, the centered-and-scaled steady state converges weakly to a Gaussian random vector. However, for fixed $α$, this weak convergence offers no usable error bound for approximating the steady-state by its Gaussian limit. This paper provides explicit, non-asymptotic error bounds for fixed $α$. We first prove general-purpose theorems that bound the Wasserstein distance between the centered-scaled steady state and an appropriate Gaussian distribution, under regularity conditions for drift and moment conditions for noise. To ensure broad applicability, we cover both i.i.d. and Markovian noise models. We then instantiate these theorems for three representative SA settings: (1) stochastic gradient descent (SGD) for smooth strongly convex objectives, (2) linear SA, and (3) contractive nonlinear SA. We obtain dimension- and stepsize-dependent, explicit bounds in Wasserstein distance of order $α^{1/2}\log(1/α)$ for small $α$. Building on the Wasserstein approximation error, we further derive non-uniform Berry--Esseen-type tail bounds that compare the steady-state tail probability to Gaussian tails. We achieve an explicit error term that decays in both the deviation level and stepsize $α$. We adapt the same analysis for SGD beyond strongly convexity and study general convex objectives. We identify a non-Gaussian (Gibbs) limiting law under the correct scaling, which is validated numerically, and provide a corresponding pre-limit Wasserstein error bound.

Verdict

Disconfirmed as written.

Let m denote the even integer from Assumption 5.1, and let φ be the test function (the paper uses h for both, which is itself ambiguous). Take φ(y)=|y|, which belongs to Lip_1(R). If there were a solution g with classical derivatives g', g'', g''' satisfying
φ(y) - E[φ(Z)] = - y^m g'(y) + E[ξ_k^2] g''(y),
then the right-hand side would be differentiable at y=0, while |y|-E|Z| is not differentiable at 0. So no such g can exist. This already refutes the exact conjecture. The paper’s own appendix also uses a different Stein operator and different target density, so the displayed conjecture appears typo-contaminated as well. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is poorly specified.

First, the symbol h is overloaded: in Section 5 it denotes both the even integer order from Assumption 5.1 and the Lipschitz test function in Conjecture 5.2. Second, Z is not defined inside the conjecture itself; the nearby text suggests it is meant to be the target Gibbs law. Third, the operator/density pair is internally inconsistent: the conjecture states -y^m g' + E[ξ_k^2]g'', the next sentence associates this with the diffusion dY_t=-Y_t^m dt+sqrt(2E[ξ_k^2])dW_t and a density proportional to exp{-|y|^{m+1}/((m+1)E[ξ_k^2])}, while Appendix E instead uses -f^{(m)}(x*)/(m-1)! y^{m-1}g' + (σ^2/2)g'' and Proposition 5.1 targets a Gibbs density proportional to exp(-c y^m). Finally, the conjecture never says whether g''' is meant classically, almost everywhere, or weakly; that distinction is decisive here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Reasoning

The counterexample is simple. Let
F(y) := - y^m g'(y) + σ^2 g''(y),
with σ^2 := E[ξ_k^2]. If g', g'', g''' exist classically, then F is differentiable at every y. In particular, since m>=2,
F'(0) = σ^2 g'''(0).
So if the Stein equation held with φ(y)=|y|, then |y|-E|Z| would be differentiable at 0, contradiction. Hence the conjecture is false even before worrying about uniform bounds.

This same regularity obstruction also kills the natural corrected operator from the appendix:
Lg(y) = -κ y^{m-1} g'(y) + (σ^2/2) g''(y).
With classical g''', Lg is still differentiable, so one still cannot solve Lg = φ - Eφ(Z) for every merely Lipschitz φ. Thus the problem is not only the power typo; the demanded regularity is too strong for the Wasserstein test class.

There is also an independent structural mismatch in the paper. As written, the diffusion dY_t=-Y_t^m dt+sqrt(2σ^2)dW_t does not match the claimed invariant density with |y|^{m+1} in the exponent. For constant diffusion coefficient, the formal zero-flux density for drift -y^m is proportional to exp(-y^{m+1}/((m+1)σ^2)), which is not normalizable when m is even. By contrast, Appendix E uses the generator
Lg(y)= -f^{(m)}(x*)/(m-1)! y^{m-1} g'(y) + (σ^2/2) g''(y),
which does match the Gibbs density p(y)∝exp(-c y^m) appearing in Proposition 5.1. So the written conjecture is not just unproved; it is inconsistent with the rest of the paper. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

A sensible repair would be: use the appendix operator, and either weaken the regularity claim to bounded g' and g'' (or bounded weak third derivative), or strengthen the test class from Lip_1 to something like C^{1,1}. For the repaired 1D Gibbs target, the standard 1D Stein machinery does provide an explicit integral solution formula via the inverse Stein operator, and later work gives strong or weak Stein-factor results under additional diffusion/Poincaré assumptions. I am not claiming a full proof of that repaired variant here; only that the right framework is already available. What definitely fails is the exact classical g''' claim for all Lipschitz tests. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.2998))

Literature overlap

For the exact conjecture as written: no. It is false, so there is no exact positive result to locate in the literature.

For nearby corrected statements: best classified as (b) close to existing literature. Ley, Reinert, and Swan give the general one-dimensional inverse Stein operator, which yields the explicit integral representation underlying corrected Gibbs Stein equations. Mijoule, Raič, Reinert, and Swan summarize that, under stronger diffusion assumptions, the corresponding strong Stein equation for Lipschitz test functions has a twice continuously differentiable solution with Stein-factor bounds; they also prove a weak Stein equation for Lipschitz tests under a Poincaré constant. These are close in spirit, but they are not the same as the paper’s written claim, and they do not rescue the classical third-derivative bound for every Lipschitz test function. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.2998))

So my classification is:
- exact conjecture: false;
- natural corrected operator: close to known 1D Stein machinery;
- natural corrected regularity claim: should be reformulated, because “classical bounded g''' for all h∈Lip_1” is too strong, whereas “bounded g', g''” or “bounded weak third derivative” is much more plausible and much closer to what is already known. Higher-order Stein-factor bounds are typically tied to smoothness of the test function itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.02623?utm_source=openai))

Sources

Wang, Wang, Narang, F. Wang, Y. Wang, Maguluri, arXiv:2602.13960v1: Conjecture 5.2 as stated, its surrounding diffusion interpretation, Proposition 5.1 target density, and Appendix E’s actual generator. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Ley, Reinert, Swan, “Stein’s method for comparison of univariate distributions” (2017): canonical inverse Stein operator / explicit 1D solution formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.2998))

Mijoule, Raič, Reinert, Swan, “Stein’s density method for multivariate continuous distributions” (2023): strong diffusion Stein-factor results under stronger assumptions and weak Stein equations under a Poincaré condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.05079))

Döbler, “An iterative technique for bounding derivatives of solutions of Stein equations”: higher-order derivative bounds in Stein’s method are formulated in terms of smoothness of the test function. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.02623?utm_source=openai))

Disconfirmed as written.

Let m denote the even integer from Assumption 5.1, and let φ be the test function (the paper uses h for both, which is itself ambiguous). Take φ(y)=|y|, which belongs to Lip_1(R). If there were a solution g with classical derivatives g', g'', g''' satisfying
φ(y) - E[φ(Z)] = - y^m g'(y) + E[ξ_k^2] g''(y),
then the right-hand side would be differentiable at y=0, while |y|-E|Z| is not differentiable at 0. So no such g can exist. This already refutes the exact conjecture. The paper’s own appendix also uses a different Stein operator and different target density, so the displayed conjecture appears typo-contaminated as well. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is poorly specified.

First, the symbol h is overloaded: in Section 5 it denotes both the even integer order from Assumption 5.1 and the Lipschitz test function in Conjecture 5.2. Second, Z is not defined inside the conjecture itself; the nearby text suggests it is meant to be the target Gibbs law. Third, the operator/density pair is internally inconsistent: the conjecture states -y^m g' + E[ξ_k^2]g'', the next sentence associates this with the diffusion dY_t=-Y_t^m dt+sqrt(2E[ξ_k^2])dW_t and a density proportional to exp{-|y|^{m+1}/((m+1)E[ξ_k^2])}, while Appendix E instead uses -f^{(m)}(x*)/(m-1)! y^{m-1}g' + (σ^2/2)g'' and Proposition 5.1 targets a Gibbs density proportional to exp(-c y^m). Finally, the conjecture never says whether g''' is meant classically, almost everywhere, or weakly; that distinction is decisive here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Reasoning

The counterexample is simple. Let
F(y) := - y^m g'(y) + σ^2 g''(y),
with σ^2 := E[ξ_k^2]. If g', g'', g''' exist classically, then F is differentiable at every y. In particular, since m>=2,
F'(0) = σ^2 g'''(0).
So if the Stein equation held with φ(y)=|y|, then |y|-E|Z| would be differentiable at 0, contradiction. Hence the conjecture is false even before worrying about uniform bounds.

This same regularity obstruction also kills the natural corrected operator from the appendix:
Lg(y) = -κ y^{m-1} g'(y) + (σ^2/2) g''(y).
With classical g''', Lg is still differentiable, so one still cannot solve Lg = φ - Eφ(Z) for every merely Lipschitz φ. Thus the problem is not only the power typo; the demanded regularity is too strong for the Wasserstein test class.

There is also an independent structural mismatch in the paper. As written, the diffusion dY_t=-Y_t^m dt+sqrt(2σ^2)dW_t does not match the claimed invariant density with |y|^{m+1} in the exponent. For constant diffusion coefficient, the formal zero-flux density for drift -y^m is proportional to exp(-y^{m+1}/((m+1)σ^2)), which is not normalizable when m is even. By contrast, Appendix E uses the generator
Lg(y)= -f^{(m)}(x*)/(m-1)! y^{m-1} g'(y) + (σ^2/2) g''(y),
which does match the Gibbs density p(y)∝exp(-c y^m) appearing in Proposition 5.1. So the written conjecture is not just unproved; it is inconsistent with the rest of the paper. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

A sensible repair would be: use the appendix operator, and either weaken the regularity claim to bounded g' and g'' (or bounded weak third derivative), or strengthen the test class from Lip_1 to something like C^{1,1}. For the repaired 1D Gibbs target, the standard 1D Stein machinery does provide an explicit integral solution formula via the inverse Stein operator, and later work gives strong or weak Stein-factor results under additional diffusion/Poincaré assumptions. I am not claiming a full proof of that repaired variant here; only that the right framework is already available. What definitely fails is the exact classical g''' claim for all Lipschitz tests. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.2998))

Literature overlap

For the exact conjecture as written: no. It is false, so there is no exact positive result to locate in the literature.

For nearby corrected statements: best classified as (b) close to existing literature. Ley, Reinert, and Swan give the general one-dimensional inverse Stein operator, which yields the explicit integral representation underlying corrected Gibbs Stein equations. Mijoule, Raič, Reinert, and Swan summarize that, under stronger diffusion assumptions, the corresponding strong Stein equation for Lipschitz test functions has a twice continuously differentiable solution with Stein-factor bounds; they also prove a weak Stein equation for Lipschitz tests under a Poincaré constant. These are close in spirit, but they are not the same as the paper’s written claim, and they do not rescue the classical third-derivative bound for every Lipschitz test function. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.2998))

So my classification is:
- exact conjecture: false;
- natural corrected operator: close to known 1D Stein machinery;
- natural corrected regularity claim: should be reformulated, because “classical bounded g''' for all h∈Lip_1” is too strong, whereas “bounded g', g''” or “bounded weak third derivative” is much more plausible and much closer to what is already known. Higher-order Stein-factor bounds are typically tied to smoothness of the test function itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.02623?utm_source=openai))

Döbler, “An iterative technique for bounding derivatives of solutions of Stein equations”: higher-order derivative bounds in Stein’s method are formulated in terms of smoothness of the test function. CONJECTURE_ID: 316
ARXIV_ID: 2602.15797v1
TITLE: On Graham's rearrangement conjecture
AUTHORS: Huy Tuan Pham, Lisa Sauermann
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15797v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.15797v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: rearrangement.tex:70-72

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Graham conjectured in 1971 that for any prime $p$, any subset $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}_p\setminus \{0\}$ admits an ordering $s_1,s_2,\dots,s_{|S|}$ where all partial sums $s_1, s_1+s_2,\dots,s_1+s_2+\dots+s_{|S|}$ are distinct. We prove this conjecture for all subsets $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}_p\setminus \{0\}$ with $|S|\le p^{1-α}$ and $|S|$ sufficiently large with respect to $α$, for any $α\in (0,1)$. Combined with earlier results, this gives a complete resolution of Graham's rearrangement conjecture for all sufficiently large primes $p$.

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
Any subset $S\su \mathbb{Z}_p\setminus \{0\}$ admits a valid ordering.

CONJECTURE_PLAIN_TEXT:
Any subset $S Z_p \ 0\ $ admits a valid ordering.

  62:

  65: \section{Introduction}
  66: 
  67: Given an abelian group $G$ and a subset $S\su G$, a \emph{valid ordering} of $S$ is an ordering $s_1,s_2,\dots,s_{|S|}$ of the elements of $S$ such that all partial sums $s_1,s_1+s_2,\dots,s_1+s_2+\dots+s_{|S|}$ are distinct. %
  68: 
  69: Valid orderings have been a subject of a number of works in combinatorics and group theory. The main subject of this paper is the a conjecture, called Graham's rearrangement conjecture, which was posed by Graham \cite[p. 36]{G} in 1971  and later reiterated by Erd\H{o}s and Graham \cite[p. 95]{EG}. 
  70: \begin{conj}
  71:     Any subset $S\su \mathbb{Z}_p\setminus \{0\}$ admits a valid ordering. 
  72: \end{conj}
  73: 
  74: Graham's rearrangement conjecture has attracted a lot of interest over the past few years. Bedert and Kravitz \cite{BK} showed that the conjecture holds true for small sets $S$, namely for $|S| \le \exp((\log p)^{1/4})$, improving an earlier result of Kravitz \cite{Kr} and an observation of Sawin \cite{Sa}. M\"uyesser and Pokrovskiy \cite{MP} showed that the conjecture holds for very large $S$ of size $|S|\ge (1-o(1))p$. Buci\'c, Frederickson, M\"uyesser, Pokrovskiy and Yepremyan \cite{BFMPY} showed an approximate version of the conjecture, where all but $o(|S|)$ many partial sums are guaranteed to be distinct. Recently, Bedert, Buci\'c, Kravitz, Montgomery and M\"uyesser \cite{BBKMM} showed that the conjecture holds true for large sets $S$, namely for $|S| \ge p^{1-c}$ for a small positive constant $c$. 
  75: 
  76: The main result of this paper is the following theorem, which shows that Graham's rearrangement conjecture holds for all $S\su \mathbb{Z}_p\setminus \{0\}$ with $C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}$, for any $\alpha>0$ (with a suitable constant $C_\alpha>0$ depending on $\alpha$).
  77: 
  78: \begin{thm}\label{thm:main}
  79:     For any $0<\alpha<1$, there exists a constant $C_\alpha>0$ such that the following holds. Let $p$ be a prime and let $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}_p \setminus \{0\}$ be a subset with $C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}$. Then there exists a valid ordering of $S$, i.e.\ there is an ordering $s_1,s_2,\dots,s_{|S|}$ of the elements of $S$ such that all partial sums $s_1,s_1+s_2,\dots,s_1+s_2+\dots+s_{|S|}$ are distinct.  
  80: \end{thm}
  81: 
  82: Together with the earlier results discussed above, this completely settles Graham's rearrangement conjecture for all sufficiently large primes $p$.
  83: 
  84: Unlike previous results towards Graham's rearrangement conjecture, employing structural additive combinatorial results, or graph-theoretic arguments relying on suitable notions of expansion and the absorption method, our proof is inherently probabilistic, relying on anticoncentration estimates on sums of random subsets of $S$ of a given size. More precisely, we prove the following anticoncentration result, which is an important input for our proof of Theorem \ref{thm:main}.
  85:

  89: \begin{thm}\label{thm:anticonc}
  90:     There exists an absolute constant $C>0$ such that the following holds. Let $p$ be a prime, let $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}_p$ be a subset of size $|S|\ge 2$, and let $m$ be an integer with $C\log |S|\le m\le 10^{-3}|S|/\log |S|$. Now, let $R\su S$ be a uniformly random subset of $S$ of size $m$, and let  $\Sigma(R) = \sum_{x\in R}x\in \mathbb{Z}_p$ be the sum of the elements of $R$. Then 
  91:     \[
  92:         \max_{z\in \mathbb{Z}_p} \mathbb{P}[\Sigma(R)=z] \le \frac{1}{p} + \frac{C}{|S|\sqrt{m}}.

Verdict

As of March 8, 2026, I cannot confirm or disconfirm Conjecture 316. The newest paper I found, Pham–Sauermann (arXiv:2602.15797v1), proves the conjecture for every fixed \(0<\alpha<1\) in the range \(C_\alpha \le |S| \le p^{1-\alpha}\); combined with Bedert–Bucić–Kravitz–Montgomery–Müyesser’s general large-subset theorem \(|S|\ge |G|^{1-c}\), this yields Graham’s conjecture for all sufficiently large primes, but not the exact all-primes statement. I found no source giving a full proof or a counterexample for the exact formulation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Well-specifiedness

In the paper, the conjecture is well specified once one includes the immediately preceding definition: a valid ordering is an ordering whose partial sums are pairwise distinct. The only mild caveat is that the isolated sentence “Any subset \(S\subseteq \mathbb Z_p\setminus\{0\}\) admits a valid ordering” is not self-contained unless that nearby definition is included, and the symbol \(\su\) relies on the paper’s notation setup. With that local context, the statement is precise. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Reasoning

A standard reduction already in the literature is that Alspach’s stronger nonzero-sum conjecture implies Graham’s conjecture in cyclic groups: if a set has total sum \(0\), delete one element, apply Alspach to the remaining nonzero-sum set, then append the deleted element. So one natural route to Conjecture 316 would be to prove the prime-order case of Alspach’s conjecture. ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Among the exact results I found, Graham’s conjecture is verified for all cyclic groups of order at most \(25\) by computation, and for prime cyclic groups of subset-size \(12\) (building on earlier exact results up to \(11\)). Later work greatly enlarges the proved ranges: Kravitz proved \(|S|\le \log p/\log\log p\) and also the integer analogue “every finite subset of \(\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}\) has a valid ordering”; Bedert–Kravitz improved the prime-field small-set range to \(|S|\le e^{(\log p)^{1/4}}\); Bedert–Bucić–Kravitz–Montgomery–Müyesser proved a general theorem for \(|S|\ge |G|^{1-c}\); and Pham–Sauermann proved the intermediate regime \(C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}\). These results are very close to the full statement, but the sources I found stop short of “for every prime \(p\).” ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

I also tried a direct upgrade from a Graham-valid ordering of a nonzero-sum set to an Alspach ordering by cyclic rotation, but the naive version fails. For example, in \(\mathbb Z_7\), the ordering \(1,6,2\) of \(\{1,2,6\}\) is Graham-valid because its partial sums are \(1,0,2\), all distinct; but rotating after the zero prefix gives \(2,1,6\), whose partial sums are \(2,3,2\), so a collision appears. So that approach does not yield a proof.

I also do not see how to finish the conjecture by a finite computation from the published asymptotic statements alone. The Pham–Sauermann theorem is stated with a parameter-dependent constant \(C_\alpha\), and the large-set theorem with an unspecified absolute \(c>0\); that is enough for “sufficiently large primes,” but not enough, from the information available in those statements, to identify and exhaust the remaining primes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Literature overlap

For the exact statement “for every prime \(p\), every subset \(S\subseteq \mathbb Z_p\setminus\{0\}\) admits a valid ordering,” my assessment is (b), not (a): a close version exists in the literature, but not the exact theorem. The closest current result is the February 17, 2026 Pham–Sauermann theorem together with the 2025 large-subset theorem, which jointly settle the conjecture for all sufficiently large primes. Exact small-prime and fixed-size results are also known, and the rectification/integer route is already well developed in Kravitz and Bedert–Kravitz. So a complete proof for all primes, or a counterexample at one of the remaining finitely many primes, would still be genuinely new relative to the sources I found. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

If one eventually proves the conjecture by first proving the prime-order case of Alspach’s stronger conjecture and then invoking the published reduction Alspach \(\Rightarrow\) Graham, that would overlap existing literature conceptually but not nearly exactly: the reduction is already in Archdeacon–Dinitz–Mattern–Stinson, but the needed full prime-order Alspach theorem is not present in the sources I found. ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Sources

Pham and Sauermann, “On Graham’s rearrangement conjecture” (arXiv:2602.15797v1): definition of valid ordering, exact conjecture statement, and the theorem \(C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Bedert, Bucić, Kravitz, Montgomery, and Müyesser, “On Graham’s rearrangement conjecture over \(\mathbb F_2^n\)” (arXiv:2508.18254): abstract states the general theorem that every subset of size at least \(|G|^{1-c}\) in any finite group admits a valid ordering. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.18254))

Archdeacon, Dinitz, Mattern, and Stinson, “On Partial Sums in Cyclic Groups”: Proposition 1.1 gives Alspach \(\Rightarrow\) Graham, and the paper reports computer verification up to cyclic order \(25\). ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Costa and Pellegrini, “Some new results about a conjecture by Brian Alspach” (2020): summarizes earlier prime-order exact results up to size \(11\) and proves the size-\(12\) Graham result. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00013-020-01507-7))

Kravitz, “Rearranging small sets for distinct partial sums” (2024): proves \(|S|\le \log p/\log\log p\) and the integer analogue. ([math.colgate.edu](https://math.colgate.edu/~integers/y113/y113.pdf))

Bedert and Kravitz, “Graham’s rearrangement conjecture beyond the rectification barrier” (2025): improves the small-set range to \(|S|\le e^{(\log p)^{1/4}}\). ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11856-025-2871-6))

As of March 8, 2026, I cannot confirm or disconfirm Conjecture 316. The newest paper I found, Pham–Sauermann (arXiv:2602.15797v1), proves the conjecture for every fixed \(0<\alpha<1\) in the range \(C_\alpha \le |S| \le p^{1-\alpha}\); combined with Bedert–Bucić–Kravitz–Montgomery–Müyesser’s general large-subset theorem \(|S|\ge |G|^{1-c}\), this yields Graham’s conjecture for all sufficiently large primes, but not the exact all-primes statement. I found no source giving a full proof or a counterexample for the exact formulation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Well-specifiedness

In the paper, the conjecture is well specified once one includes the immediately preceding definition: a valid ordering is an ordering whose partial sums are pairwise distinct. The only mild caveat is that the isolated sentence “Any subset \(S\subseteq \mathbb Z_p\setminus\{0\}\) admits a valid ordering” is not self-contained unless that nearby definition is included, and the symbol \(\su\) relies on the paper’s notation setup. With that local context, the statement is precise. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Reasoning

A standard reduction already in the literature is that Alspach’s stronger nonzero-sum conjecture implies Graham’s conjecture in cyclic groups: if a set has total sum \(0\), delete one element, apply Alspach to the remaining nonzero-sum set, then append the deleted element. So one natural route to Conjecture 316 would be to prove the prime-order case of Alspach’s conjecture. ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Among the exact results I found, Graham’s conjecture is verified for all cyclic groups of order at most \(25\) by computation, and for prime cyclic groups of subset-size \(12\) (building on earlier exact results up to \(11\)). Later work greatly enlarges the proved ranges: Kravitz proved \(|S|\le \log p/\log\log p\) and also the integer analogue “every finite subset of \(\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}\) has a valid ordering”; Bedert–Kravitz improved the prime-field small-set range to \(|S|\le e^{(\log p)^{1/4}}\); Bedert–Bucić–Kravitz–Montgomery–Müyesser proved a general theorem for \(|S|\ge |G|^{1-c}\); and Pham–Sauermann proved the intermediate regime \(C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}\). These results are very close to the full statement, but the sources I found stop short of “for every prime \(p\).” ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

I also tried a direct upgrade from a Graham-valid ordering of a nonzero-sum set to an Alspach ordering by cyclic rotation, but the naive version fails. For example, in \(\mathbb Z_7\), the ordering \(1,6,2\) of \(\{1,2,6\}\) is Graham-valid because its partial sums are \(1,0,2\), all distinct; but rotating after the zero prefix gives \(2,1,6\), whose partial sums are \(2,3,2\), so a collision appears. So that approach does not yield a proof.

I also do not see how to finish the conjecture by a finite computation from the published asymptotic statements alone. The Pham–Sauermann theorem is stated with a parameter-dependent constant \(C_\alpha\), and the large-set theorem with an unspecified absolute \(c>0\); that is enough for “sufficiently large primes,” but not enough, from the information available in those statements, to identify and exhaust the remaining primes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Literature overlap

For the exact statement “for every prime \(p\), every subset \(S\subseteq \mathbb Z_p\setminus\{0\}\) admits a valid ordering,” my assessment is (b), not (a): a close version exists in the literature, but not the exact theorem. The closest current result is the February 17, 2026 Pham–Sauermann theorem together with the 2025 large-subset theorem, which jointly settle the conjecture for all sufficiently large primes. Exact small-prime and fixed-size results are also known, and the rectification/integer route is already well developed in Kravitz and Bedert–Kravitz. So a complete proof for all primes, or a counterexample at one of the remaining finitely many primes, would still be genuinely new relative to the sources I found. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

If one eventually proves the conjecture by first proving the prime-order case of Alspach’s stronger conjecture and then invoking the published reduction Alspach \(\Rightarrow\) Graham, that would overlap existing literature conceptually but not nearly exactly: the reduction is already in Archdeacon–Dinitz–Mattern–Stinson, but the needed full prime-order Alspach theorem is not present in the sources I found. ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Sources

Pham and Sauermann, “On Graham’s rearrangement conjecture” (arXiv:2602.15797v1): definition of valid ordering, exact conjecture statement, and the theorem \(C_\alpha\le |S|\le p^{1-\alpha}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.15797v1))

Bedert, Bucić, Kravitz, Montgomery, and Müyesser, “On Graham’s rearrangement conjecture over \(\mathbb F_2^n\)” (arXiv:2508.18254): abstract states the general theorem that every subset of size at least \(|G|^{1-c}\) in any finite group admits a valid ordering. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.18254))

Archdeacon, Dinitz, Mattern, and Stinson, “On Partial Sums in Cyclic Groups”: Proposition 1.1 gives Alspach \(\Rightarrow\) Graham, and the paper reports computer verification up to cyclic order \(25\). ([cs.uwaterloo.ca](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/~dstinson/papers/J256.pdf))

Costa and Pellegrini, “Some new results about a conjecture by Brian Alspach” (2020): summarizes earlier prime-order exact results up to size \(11\) and proves the size-\(12\) Graham result. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00013-020-01507-7))

Kravitz, “Rearranging small sets for distinct partial sums” (2024): proves \(|S|\le \log p/\log\log p\) and the integer analogue. ([math.colgate.edu](https://math.colgate.edu/~integers/y113/y113.pdf))

Bedert and Kravitz, “Graham’s rearrangement conjecture beyond the rectification barrier” (2025): improves the small-set range to \(|S|\le e^{(\log p)^{1/4}}\). CONJECTURE_ID: 124
ARXIV_ID: 2602.23066v1
TITLE: Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups
AUTHORS: Zach Walsh
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23066v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.23066v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: main.tex:2306-2308

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
We present a new construction for matroids from gain graphs that simultaneously generalizes several existing constructions. The construction takes as input a gain graph over a Frobenius group $Γ$ with Frobenius kernel $Γ_1$ and outputs an elementary lift of the frame matroid of the underlying gain graph over the quotient group $Γ/Γ_1$. While the hypothesis that $Γ$ is a Frobenius group may seem unusual, we prove that it is in some sense necessary: if $Γ$ is any finite group with a nontrivial proper normal subgroup $Γ_1$ and there is a construction that takes in a complete $Γ$-gain graph and outputs an elementary lift $M$ of the frame matroid of the underlying $(Γ/Γ_1)$-gain graph so that a cycle of the graph is a circuit of $M$ if and only if it is $Γ$-balanced, then $Γ$ is a Frobenius group with Frobenius kernel $Γ_1$.

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
If $\Gamma$ is a Frobenius group with Frobenius kernel $\Gamma_1$ and set $\cA$ of Frobenius complements, then $\cM(\Gamma, \Gamma_1, \cA)$ has a sequence of universal models.

CONJECTURE_PLAIN_TEXT:
If $$ is a Frobenius group with Frobenius kernel $_1$ and set $$ of Frobenius complements, then $(, _1, )$ has a sequence of universal models.

2292: \section{Future work} \label{sec: future work}
2293: While we have already presented several conjectures (Conjectures \ref{conj: excluded minors infinite case}, \ref{conj: excluded minors finite case}, \ref{conj: equivalence for canonical matrices}, and \ref{conj: representable implies incidence matrix of gain function}) and one problem (Problem \ref{prob: infinite group partition properties}), we close by discussing several more interesting directions for future work.

2299: \subsection{Universal models}
2300: Following Kahn and Kung \cite{Kahn-Kung1982}, a \emph{sequence of universal models} for a class $\cM$ of matroids is a sequence $M_2, M_3, \dots$ of simple matroids in $\cM$ so that for each $r \ge 2$, the matroid $M_r$ has rank $r$ and every simple rank-$r$ matroid in $\cM$ is a restriction of $M_r$.
2301: For example, projective geometries form a sequence of universal models for matroids representable over a given finite field.
2302: It is well-known that for every finite group $\Gamma$, the classes of $\Gamma$-frame matroids and $\Gamma$-lifted-graphic matroids each have a sequence of universal models (see \cite[page 47]{Zaslavsky1991} and \cite[Chapter 4]{Geelen-Nelson-Walsh-2024}).
2303: This should be true more generally for $\cM(\Gamma, \Gamma_1, \cA)$ as well when $\Gamma$ is a Frobenius group.

2306: \begin{conjecture}
2307: If $\Gamma$ is a Frobenius group with Frobenius kernel $\Gamma_1$ and set $\cA$ of Frobenius complements, then $\cM(\Gamma, \Gamma_1, \cA)$ has a sequence of universal models.
2308: \end{conjecture}
2309: 
2310: We plan to prove this conjecture in a sequel paper, and to show that the universal models are unavoidable minors of dense elementary lifts of frame matroids known as $(\alpha, 1)$-frame matroids, which are conjectured by Geelen, Gerards, and Whittle \cite[Conjecure 7.5]{Structure_Theory} to be of fundamental importance in extremal matroid theory.

2318: 
2319: A matroid $M$ is a \emph{rank-$t$ lift} of a matroid $N$ if there is a matroid $K$ with a set $X$ so that $K \del X = M$, $K/X = N$, and $r(M) - r(N) = t$.
2320: Note that an elementary lift is a rank-$t$ lift with $t \in \{0,1\}$.
2321: For a group $\Gamma$, Theorem \ref{thm: main} takes in a $\Gamma$-graph $(G, \psi)$ and outputs an elementary lift $M$ of the frame matroid $F(G, \psi)$ so that a cycle of $G$ is a circuit of $M$ if and only if it is $\psi$-balanced.
2322: What if we wish to construct a rank-$t$ lift with $t \ge 2$?

2326: Let $t$ be an integer with $t \ge 2$.
2327: For which finite groups $\Gamma$ with nontrivial normal subgroup $\Gamma_1$ is there a construction that takes in a $\Gamma$-gain graph $(G, \psi)$ and outputs a rank-$t$ lift of the frame matroid $F(G, \psi/\Gamma_1)$ so that a cycle of $G$ is a circuit of $M$ if and only if it is $\psi$-balanced?
2328: \end{problem}
output_text:
Verdict

I cannot confirm or disconfirm Conjecture 124 from the paper and currently available literature. A natural candidate universal family is a Dowling-/complete-gain-graph-type construction, but I do not see a complete proof, and I did not find an explicit counterexample. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is reasonably well specified once one imports Definition 4.1, which defines the class \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\), together with Section 7.1’s definition of a “sequence of universal models.” In this paper, a Frobenius group is finite, and Proposition 2.9 makes the Frobenius kernel unique and identifies \(\mathcal A\) as the set of Frobenius complements, so the data \((\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) are well defined in the finite case. A mild omission is that any concrete universal model will almost certainly have to be stated after simplification, since the universal models are required to be simple matroids. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Reasoning

The obvious route to a proof is an embedding argument into a fixed complete \(\Gamma\)-gain graph, because deletion of edges in the gain graph gives deletion/restriction of the associated matroid. Also, Lemma 3.5 gives exactly the sort of flexibility one would want in such a normalization: nontrivial \(\Gamma_1\)-loops can be moved between vertices without changing the matroid, and changing a loop label within a fixed complement subgroup does not change the matroid either. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

What I could not prove is the key normal-form step. Proposition 4.2 gives
\(r(X)=|V(G[X])|-b(X)+l(X)\), with \(l(X)\in\{0,1\}\). That formula shows that a rank-\(r\) matroid can easily have a representation on more than \(r\) vertices whenever balanced tree components occur. So a proof cannot just take an arbitrary representation and place it inside a complete gain graph; one needs a genuine re-representation theorem saying that every simple rank-\(r\) matroid in \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) can be compressed to a bounded “basis-vertex” model, analogous to the normal forms behind Dowling geometries and the known lifted-graphic universal models. I do not see that theorem in the paper, and I could not derive it from the current lemmas. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

The conjecture still looks plausible. Walsh explicitly frames it as the expected extension of the already-known universal-model results for finite-group frame matroids and finite-group lifted-graphic matroids, and Proposition 4.4 shows that the mixed class contains all \(\Gamma_1\)-lifted-graphic matroids and all \(A\)-frame matroids for \(A\in\mathcal A\). So the conjecture fits the surrounding theory very naturally. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

The representable subfamily in Theorem 5.6 also points in the same direction: there the matroids \(M(F^+\rtimes F^\times,F^+,\mathcal A,G,\psi)\) are represented by matrices obtained by adding one row to a frame matrix, which strongly suggests a “take all allowable columns” universal construction in finite-field cases. But that is only suggestive; it is not a proof for the full class. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

I am also cautious because nearby lift-universality conjectures have turned out to be delicate: Bernstein and Walsh show that a different general conjecture about matroid lifts is true for representable matroids but false in general. That does not refute the present conjecture, but it is enough that I do not want to claim success without a full normalization argument. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12543))

Literature overlap

I did not find the exact theorem already proved elsewhere, so I would not classify this as (a) “nearly exact in the literature.”

The best fit is (b) “a close version exists in the literature.” Walsh explicitly states that, for every finite group \(\Gamma\), the classes of \(\Gamma\)-frame matroids and \(\Gamma\)-lifted-graphic matroids have sequences of universal models, and Proposition 4.3 identifies those as the two extreme special cases of the present construction. Proposition 4.4 further shows that \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) contains the corresponding kernel-lifted and complement-frame subfamilies. So the conjecture is a natural interpolation of known universal-model phenomena, but I did not locate the exact mixed Frobenius-kernel/complement statement itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

So my novelty assessment is: exact statement likely new; closest existing results are strong special cases, not the same theorem. That is closer to (b) than to (c), but only because the neighboring special cases are already very close. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Sources

Walsh, “Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups,” arXiv:2602.23066v1: definitions of Frobenius groups/partitions, Definition 4.1 of \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\), Proposition 4.2 (rank formula), Proposition 4.3–4.4 (special cases/subclasses), Lemma 3.5 (loop moves), Theorem 4.10 (minor-closure), Theorem 5.6 (representable subfamily), and Conjecture 7.1 itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Bernstein–Walsh, “Matroid lifts and representability,” arXiv:2306.12543v2: cautionary nearby example showing a general lift conjecture can hold representably yet fail in full generality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12543))

I cannot confirm or disconfirm Conjecture 124 from the paper and currently available literature. A natural candidate universal family is a Dowling-/complete-gain-graph-type construction, but I do not see a complete proof, and I did not find an explicit counterexample. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is reasonably well specified once one imports Definition 4.1, which defines the class \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\), together with Section 7.1’s definition of a “sequence of universal models.” In this paper, a Frobenius group is finite, and Proposition 2.9 makes the Frobenius kernel unique and identifies \(\mathcal A\) as the set of Frobenius complements, so the data \((\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) are well defined in the finite case. A mild omission is that any concrete universal model will almost certainly have to be stated after simplification, since the universal models are required to be simple matroids. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Reasoning

The obvious route to a proof is an embedding argument into a fixed complete \(\Gamma\)-gain graph, because deletion of edges in the gain graph gives deletion/restriction of the associated matroid. Also, Lemma 3.5 gives exactly the sort of flexibility one would want in such a normalization: nontrivial \(\Gamma_1\)-loops can be moved between vertices without changing the matroid, and changing a loop label within a fixed complement subgroup does not change the matroid either. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

What I could not prove is the key normal-form step. Proposition 4.2 gives
\(r(X)=|V(G[X])|-b(X)+l(X)\), with \(l(X)\in\{0,1\}\). That formula shows that a rank-\(r\) matroid can easily have a representation on more than \(r\) vertices whenever balanced tree components occur. So a proof cannot just take an arbitrary representation and place it inside a complete gain graph; one needs a genuine re-representation theorem saying that every simple rank-\(r\) matroid in \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) can be compressed to a bounded “basis-vertex” model, analogous to the normal forms behind Dowling geometries and the known lifted-graphic universal models. I do not see that theorem in the paper, and I could not derive it from the current lemmas. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

The conjecture still looks plausible. Walsh explicitly frames it as the expected extension of the already-known universal-model results for finite-group frame matroids and finite-group lifted-graphic matroids, and Proposition 4.4 shows that the mixed class contains all \(\Gamma_1\)-lifted-graphic matroids and all \(A\)-frame matroids for \(A\in\mathcal A\). So the conjecture fits the surrounding theory very naturally. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

The representable subfamily in Theorem 5.6 also points in the same direction: there the matroids \(M(F^+\rtimes F^\times,F^+,\mathcal A,G,\psi)\) are represented by matrices obtained by adding one row to a frame matrix, which strongly suggests a “take all allowable columns” universal construction in finite-field cases. But that is only suggestive; it is not a proof for the full class. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

I am also cautious because nearby lift-universality conjectures have turned out to be delicate: Bernstein and Walsh show that a different general conjecture about matroid lifts is true for representable matroids but false in general. That does not refute the present conjecture, but it is enough that I do not want to claim success without a full normalization argument. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12543))

Literature overlap

I did not find the exact theorem already proved elsewhere, so I would not classify this as (a) “nearly exact in the literature.”

The best fit is (b) “a close version exists in the literature.” Walsh explicitly states that, for every finite group \(\Gamma\), the classes of \(\Gamma\)-frame matroids and \(\Gamma\)-lifted-graphic matroids have sequences of universal models, and Proposition 4.3 identifies those as the two extreme special cases of the present construction. Proposition 4.4 further shows that \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\) contains the corresponding kernel-lifted and complement-frame subfamilies. So the conjecture is a natural interpolation of known universal-model phenomena, but I did not locate the exact mixed Frobenius-kernel/complement statement itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

So my novelty assessment is: exact statement likely new; closest existing results are strong special cases, not the same theorem. That is closer to (b) than to (c), but only because the neighboring special cases are already very close. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Sources

Walsh, “Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups,” arXiv:2602.23066v1: definitions of Frobenius groups/partitions, Definition 4.1 of \(\mathcal M(\Gamma,\Gamma_1,\mathcal A)\), Proposition 4.2 (rank formula), Proposition 4.3–4.4 (special cases/subclasses), Lemma 3.5 (loop moves), Theorem 4.10 (minor-closure), Theorem 5.6 (representable subfamily), and Conjecture 7.1 itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.23066v1))

Bernstein–Walsh, “Matroid lifts and representability,” arXiv:2306.12543v2: cautionary nearby example showing a general lift conjecture can hold representably yet fail in full generality. CONJECTURE_ID: 756
ARXIV_ID: 2603.04271v1
TITLE: Continuity of Magnitude at Skew Finite Subsets of $\ell_1^N$
AUTHORS: Sara Kalisnik, Davorin Lesnik
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04271v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2603.04271v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: main_magnitude_continuity.tex:1105-1109

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Magnitude is an isometric invariant of metric spaces introduced by Leinster. Although magnitude is nowhere continuous on the Gromov-Hausdorff space of finite metric spaces, continuity results are possible if we restrict the ambient space. In this paper, we focus on $\ell_1^N$ and prove that magnitude is continuous at every skew finite subset of $\ell_1^N$, that is, at every finite set whose coordinate projections are injective. For such sets, we analyze cubical thickenings and derive an explicit formula for their weight measures. This yields a formula for the magnitude of these thickenings, which we use to prove that their magnitude converges to that of the underlying finite set. Since skew finite subsets of $\ell_1^N$ form an open and dense subset of the space of all finite subsets, magnitude is continuous on an open dense subset of the space of finite subsets of $\ell_1^N$.

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
For every skew $F \in \ifsub(\ell_1^N)$ and $r \in \intoo{0}{\frac{\skw(F)}{2}}$ we have
\[\det\big(Z_{\cc_F(r)^{(0)}}\big) = 4^k r^k + O(r^{k+1})\]
where $k := 2^{N-1} N \cdot \card{F}$ (i.e.\ $k$ is the number of $1$-dimensional faces in~$\cc_F(r)$).

CONJECTURE_PLAIN_TEXT:
For every skew $F (_1^N)$ and $r 0 (F) 2 $ we have \[(Z_ _F(r)^ (0) ) = 4^k r^k + O(r^ k+1 )\] where $k := 2^ N-1 N F$ (i.e.\ $k$ is the number of $1$-dimensional faces in~$_F(r)$).

1100: For the strategy of the proof of Lemma~\ref{lemma:coefficient-limit}, it was important to use the Corner System. 1113: \begin{theorem}\label{theorem:skew-magnitude-continuity}
1114: For every $N \in \NN$, the map $\mg[\icsub(\ell_1^N)]$ is continuous at every skew input.

1117: \[\lim_{r \decr 0} \mg\big(\cc_F(r)\big) = \mg(F),\]
1118: so magnitude is continuous at~$F$ by~Corollary~\ref{corollary:continuity-of-magnitude-with-cubes}.
1119: \end{theorem}

Verdict

Disconfirmed. Conjecture 6.3 says that for every skew finite F in ℓ_1^N and every 0<r<skew(F)/2,
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})=4^k r^k+O(r^{k+1}),
with k=2^{N-1}N|F|. In N=1, the set F={0,1} is skew, skew(F)=1, and k=2. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1))

For 0<r<1/2, C_F(r)^{(0)}={-r,r,1-r,1+r}. A direct computation gives
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})=(1-e^{-4r})^2(1-e^{-2(1-2r)})
=16(1-e^{-2})r^2+O(r^3),
and 16(1-e^{-2})≠16. So the conjectured leading coefficient is wrong, hence the conjecture is false.

Well-specifiedness

Inside the paper, the conjecture is reasonably well-specified: the paper defines skewness, cubical thickenings C_F(r)=⋃_{p∈F}C_p(r) with C_p(r)=B_∞(p,r), and identifies k as the number of 1-dimensional faces. As a standalone snippet, though, it is not fully self-contained, because notation such as Fin+(ℓ_1^N), Z_X, C_F(r), and skew(F) is imported from earlier sections, and the O(r^{k+1}) is only implicitly an asymptotic as r→0^+. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1))

Reasoning

In dimension 1, a skew finite set is just a finite set of distinct real numbers. Write F={x_1<⋯<x_m}. Then C_F(r) is the union of the disjoint intervals [x_i-r,x_i+r], so its vertex set is
y_1=x_1-r<y_2=x_1+r<y_3=x_2-r<⋯<y_{2m}=x_m+r
whenever 0<r<skew(F)/2. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1))

Now take any ordered points y_1<⋯<y_n on the real line and set q_i:=e^{-(y_{i+1}-y_i)}. For the similarity matrix Z=(e^{-|y_i-y_j|})_{i,j=1}^n, do the descending row operations
R_i ← R_i - q_{i-1}R_{i-1},  i=n,n-1,…,2.
If j<i, then
Z_{ij}=e^{-(y_i-y_j)}=e^{-(y_i-y_{i-1})}e^{-(y_{i-1}-y_j)}=q_{i-1}Z_{i-1,j},
so the new (i,j)-entry is 0.
If j≥i, then
Z_{i-1,j}=e^{-(y_j-y_{i-1})}=q_{i-1}e^{-(y_j-y_i)}=q_{i-1}Z_{ij},
so the new (i,j)-entry is (1-q_{i-1}^2)Z_{ij}.
Hence the matrix becomes upper triangular with diagonal entries
1, 1-q_1^2, …, 1-q_{n-1}^2.
Therefore
det Z = ∏_{i=1}^{n-1}(1-q_i^2).

Apply this to the 2m vertices of C_F(r). Their successive gaps are
2r, x_2-x_1-2r, 2r, x_3-x_2-2r, …, 2r.
So
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})
= (1-e^{-4r})^m ∏_{i=1}^{m-1}(1-e^{-2(x_{i+1}-x_i-2r)}).

Expanding at r=0 gives
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})
= 4^m det(Z_F) r^m + O(r^{m+1}),
where Z_F is the similarity matrix of F. Thus in N=1 the exponent k=m is correct, but the coefficient is generally 4^m det(Z_F), not 4^m. So every skew F⊂ℓ_1^1 with more than one point is a counterexample to the stated coefficient.

Literature overlap

The exact conjecture is not already known in the literature in any positive sense, because it is false. But the determinant computation behind the counterexample is very close to classical one-dimensional exponential-kernel / Kac-Murdock-Szegő calculations. Narayan and Shastry state the equally spaced Toeplitz case det(C)=(1-e^{-2κ})^N and note that it follows by Gauss triangulation, while Delgado and Peña record the modern KMS determinant formula det(A)=(1-σρ)^{n-1}. Narayan and Shastry also explicitly discuss a generalized nonuniform-spacing matrix of the same exponential form. So I would classify the overlap as (b): a close version exists in the literature. The specific observation that this conjecture already fails in N=1 appears not to be recorded in the sources I found, but it is an elementary consequence of this classical 1D determinant structure. ([osti.gov](https://www.osti.gov/pages/servlets/purl/1774560))

Sources

- Kalisnik and Lesnik, “Continuity of Magnitude at Skew Finite Subsets of ℓ_1^N,” arXiv:2603.04271v1, for the conjecture and its notation/definitions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04271v1))
- Narayan and Shastry, “Generalized Toeplitz–Hankel matrices and their application to a layered electron gas” (2021), for classical determinant formulas of exponential-kernel matrices and the generalized nonuniform-spacing setup. ([osti.gov](https://www.osti.gov/pages/servlets/purl/1774560))
- Delgado and Peña, “High-relative-accuracy computations with Kac-Murdock-Szegö matrices and their generalizations” (2025), for a modern statement of KMS determinant formulas. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40314-025-03137-7))

det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})=(1-e^{-4r})^2(1-e^{-2(1-2r)})
=16(1-e^{-2})r^2+O(r^3),
and 16(1-e^{-2})≠16. So the conjectured leading coefficient is wrong, hence the conjecture is false.

Well-specifiedness

Inside the paper, the conjecture is reasonably well-specified: the paper defines skewness, cubical thickenings C_F(r)=⋃_{p∈F}C_p(r) with C_p(r)=B_∞(p,r), and identifies k as the number of 1-dimensional faces. As a standalone snippet, though, it is not fully self-contained, because notation such as Fin+(ℓ_1^N), Z_X, C_F(r), and skew(F) is imported from earlier sections, and the O(r^{k+1}) is only implicitly an asymptotic as r→0^+. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1))

Reasoning

In dimension 1, a skew finite set is just a finite set of distinct real numbers. Write F={x_1<⋯<x_m}. Then C_F(r) is the union of the disjoint intervals [x_i-r,x_i+r], so its vertex set is
y_1=x_1-r<y_2=x_1+r<y_3=x_2-r<⋯<y_{2m}=x_m+r
whenever 0<r<skew(F)/2. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04271v1))

Now take any ordered points y_1<⋯<y_n on the real line and set q_i:=e^{-(y_{i+1}-y_i)}. For the similarity matrix Z=(e^{-|y_i-y_j|})_{i,j=1}^n, do the descending row operations
R_i ← R_i - q_{i-1}R_{i-1},  i=n,n-1,…,2.
If j<i, then
Z_{ij}=e^{-(y_i-y_j)}=e^{-(y_i-y_{i-1})}e^{-(y_{i-1}-y_j)}=q_{i-1}Z_{i-1,j},
so the new (i,j)-entry is 0.
If j≥i, then
Z_{i-1,j}=e^{-(y_j-y_{i-1})}=q_{i-1}e^{-(y_j-y_i)}=q_{i-1}Z_{ij},
so the new (i,j)-entry is (1-q_{i-1}^2)Z_{ij}.
Hence the matrix becomes upper triangular with diagonal entries
1, 1-q_1^2, …, 1-q_{n-1}^2.
Therefore
det Z = ∏_{i=1}^{n-1}(1-q_i^2).

Apply this to the 2m vertices of C_F(r). Their successive gaps are
2r, x_2-x_1-2r, 2r, x_3-x_2-2r, …, 2r.
So
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})
= (1-e^{-4r})^m ∏_{i=1}^{m-1}(1-e^{-2(x_{i+1}-x_i-2r)}).

Expanding at r=0 gives
det(Z_{C_F(r)^{(0)}})
= 4^m det(Z_F) r^m + O(r^{m+1}),
where Z_F is the similarity matrix of F. Thus in N=1 the exponent k=m is correct, but the coefficient is generally 4^m det(Z_F), not 4^m. So every skew F⊂ℓ_1^1 with more than one point is a counterexample to the stated coefficient.

Literature overlap

The exact conjecture is not already known in the literature in any positive sense, because it is false. But the determinant computation behind the counterexample is very close to classical one-dimensional exponential-kernel / Kac-Murdock-Szegő calculations. Narayan and Shastry state the equally spaced Toeplitz case det(C)=(1-e^{-2κ})^N and note that it follows by Gauss triangulation, while Delgado and Peña record the modern KMS determinant formula det(A)=(1-σρ)^{n-1}. Narayan and Shastry also explicitly discuss a generalized nonuniform-spacing matrix of the same exponential form. So I would classify the overlap as (b): a close version exists in the literature. The specific observation that this conjecture already fails in N=1 appears not to be recorded in the sources I found, but it is an elementary consequence of this classical 1D determinant structure. ([osti.gov](https://www.osti.gov/pages/servlets/purl/1774560))

Sources

- Kalisnik and Lesnik, “Continuity of Magnitude at Skew Finite Subsets of ℓ_1^N,” arXiv:2603.04271v1, for the conjecture and its notation/definitions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04271v1))
- Narayan and Shastry, “Generalized Toeplitz–Hankel matrices and their application to a layered electron gas” (2021), for classical determinant formulas of exponential-kernel matrices and the generalized nonuniform-spacing setup. ([osti.gov](https://www.osti.gov/pages/servlets/purl/1774560))
- Delgado and Peña, “High-relative-accuracy computations with Kac-Murdock-Szegö matrices and their generalizations” (2025), for a modern statement of KMS determinant formulas. PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Let $n\geq m$ be two positive integers, $S_{n,m}=K[x_1,\ldots,x_n,y_1,\ldots,y_m]$ and $I_{n,m}=(x_iy_j\;:\;1\leq i\leq n,1\leq j\leq m)\subset S_{n,m}$ the edge ideal of a complete bipartite graph. Denote $h(n,m)=\operatorname{hdepth}(S_{n,m}/I_{n,m})$. We prove that $h(n,m)\geq \left\lceil \frac{n}{2} \right\rceil$ and the equality holds if $m$ belong to a certain interval centered in $\left\lceil \frac{n} {2} \right\rceil$. Also, we find some tight bounds for $h(n,n)$ and we prove several inequalities between $h(n,m)$ and $h(n,m')$.
