Verdict

Disconfirmed. In the paper, Assumption 5.1 allows any even \(h>0\), including \(h=2\), and Conjecture 5.1 asserts that under Assumptions 5.1–5.2 the SGD recursion (3.4),
\(X_{k+1}^{(\alpha)}=X_k^{(\alpha)}+\alpha(-f'(X_k^{(\alpha)})+\xi_k)\),
has a limiting stationary law \(X^{(\alpha)}\) for all sufficiently small \(\alpha\) and satisfies
\(\sup_{\alpha<a}\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}-x^\*|^{3h}\le C_h\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Take
\(f(x)=x^2/2\), so \(x^\*=0\) and \(h=2\).
Let \(\{\xi_k\}\) be i.i.d. with symmetric density
\(p(x)=2|x|^{-5}\mathbf 1_{\{|x|\ge 1\}}\).
Then \(\mathbb E\xi_k=0\) by symmetry,
\(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^3=4<\infty\),
but \(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^6=\infty\).
The recursion becomes
\(X_{k+1}^{(\alpha)}=(1-\alpha)X_k^{(\alpha)}+\alpha \xi_k\).
For every \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), \(X_k^{(\alpha)}\) converges in distribution to the stationary AR(1) law
\(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\).
So the existence half is true in this example. However, if
\(R_\alpha:=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 1}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\),
then \(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\) with \(R_\alpha\) independent of \(\xi_0\) and \(\mathbb E R_\alpha=0\), hence by conditional Jensen,
\[
\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}|^6
\ge
\mathbb E\big|\mathbb E[X^{(\alpha)}\mid \xi_0]\big|^6
=
\alpha^6\mathbb E|\xi_0|^6
=
\infty.
\]
Since \(3h=6\), the conjectured moment bound fails for every \(\alpha>0\). Therefore the conjecture is false.

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is not fully well-specified. Assumption 5.1 says \(f\) is of class \(C^h\) but also requires bounded \(f^{(h+1)}\), so formally it is really asking for at least \(C^{h+1}\). Assumption 5.2 is written as \(\mathbb E[\xi_k^3]<\infty\), while the surrounding prose discusses \(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^3<\infty\). More importantly, Assumption 5.2 and Conjecture 5.1 do not explicitly restate the mean-zero condition \(\mathbb E\xi_k=0\) from the earlier i.i.d.-noise Assumption 3.1, even though (3.4) was introduced in that centered-noise setting. Also, because \(h=2\) is explicitly allowed, any correct formulation must pass the quadratic test case above. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

If the conjecture is read literally without a centered-noise assumption, the existence half can fail even more directly: for example, \(f(x)=\log\cosh x\) satisfies Assumption 5.1 with \(h=2\), but with deterministic noise \(\xi_k\equiv 2\) one gets
\(X_{k+1}=X_k+\alpha(2-\tanh X_k)\ge X_k+\alpha\),
so \(X_k\to\infty\) and there is no limiting stationary distribution. So the omission of centering is mathematically consequential, not cosmetic.

Reasoning

The core obstruction is a moment mismatch. In the allowed \(h=2\) case, the conjecture demands a sixth stationary moment from only a third noise moment. For the linear quadratic recursion, the stationary law still contains the current innovation linearly, so any missing sixth moment of the noise is inherited by the stationary distribution.

Here is the verification of the first part. For \(\alpha\in(0,1)\),
\[
X_k=(1-\alpha)^kX_0+\alpha\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_{k-1-j}.
\]
Because \(\mathbb E|\xi_0|<\infty\) and \(\sum_{j\ge 0}\alpha(1-\alpha)^j=1\), the infinite series
\(\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\)
converges absolutely almost surely and in \(L^1\). Thus
\(X_k\Rightarrow X^{(\alpha)}:=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\).
So the counterexample does not attack the existence claim; it attacks the claimed \(3h\)-moment bound.

The second part fails because higher moments are impossible without corresponding innovation moments. Writing
\(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\)
with \(R_\alpha\) independent of \(\xi_0\) and mean zero,
\[
\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}|^6
=
\mathbb E\!\left[\mathbb E\!\left(|\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha|^6\mid \xi_0\right)\right]
\ge
\mathbb E\big|\mathbb E[\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\mid \xi_0]\big|^6
=
\alpha^6\mathbb E|\xi_0|^6
=
\infty.
\]
So there is no finite \(C_h\), even for a single \(\alpha\), let alone uniformly over \(\alpha\).

A plausible repair would need at least two changes: add centered noise explicitly, and replace the third-moment hypothesis by a moment assumption strong enough to control the desired \(3h\)-th stationary moment, e.g. \(\mathbb E|\xi|^{3h}<\infty\). For a general positive theorem, one would also typically expect a global dissipativity/coercivity condition, not only the local flat-minimum condition in Assumption 5.1. That last sentence is an inference about what a corrected theorem would likely need, guided by nearby dissipativity-based constant-step SGD results rather than a proof of a repaired version. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.07904))

Literature overlap

Best classification: (b) close versions exist in the literature. It cannot be (a) “nearly exact in the literature,” because the exact statement is false. The surrounding theory is already substantial: the present paper proves invariant-distribution existence in the smooth strongly convex case; Chen–Mou–Maguluri study stationary behavior in smooth strongly convex, linear, and contractive settings; Yu–Balasubramanian–Volgushev–Erdogdu analyze constant-step SGD around a unique invariant distribution under dissipativity; Bianchi–Hachem–Schechtman study invariant distributions of constant-step SGD kernels; and Azizian–Iutzeler–Malick–Mertikopoulos give Boltzmann–Gibbs-type long-run descriptions of SGD via large deviations. None of these sources imply the exact Conjecture 5.1 from only Assumptions 5.1–5.2, and the \(h=2\) counterexample explains why. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

I did not find this exact heavy-tail \(h=2\) counterexample written verbatim in the literature. But it is an elementary linear-SA/AR(1) obstruction, so I would not call the disproof itself highly novel; the broader invariant-measure/stability ideas are already well represented in nearby work.

Sources

Primary paper analyzed: Wang, Wang, Narang, Wang, Wang, and Maguluri, arXiv:2602.13960v1, especially Assumptions 5.1–5.2, Conjecture 5.1, recursion (3.4), and Proposition 3.1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Close comparison literature consulted: Chen–Mou–Maguluri on stationary behavior of constant-stepsize SGD/SA; Yu–Balasubramanian–Volgushev–Erdogdu on constant-step SGD under dissipativity; Bianchi–Hachem–Schechtman on invariant distributions for constant-step SGD; Azizian–Iutzeler–Malick–Mertikopoulos on Boltzmann–Gibbs-type long-run distributions of SGD. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.06328))

Disconfirmed. In the paper, Assumption 5.1 allows any even \(h>0\), including \(h=2\), and Conjecture 5.1 asserts that under Assumptions 5.1–5.2 the SGD recursion (3.4),
\(X_{k+1}^{(\alpha)}=X_k^{(\alpha)}+\alpha(-f'(X_k^{(\alpha)})+\xi_k)\),
has a limiting stationary law \(X^{(\alpha)}\) for all sufficiently small \(\alpha\) and satisfies
\(\sup_{\alpha<a}\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}-x^\*|^{3h}\le C_h\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Take
\(f(x)=x^2/2\), so \(x^\*=0\) and \(h=2\).
Let \(\{\xi_k\}\) be i.i.d. with symmetric density
\(p(x)=2|x|^{-5}\mathbf 1_{\{|x|\ge 1\}}\).
Then \(\mathbb E\xi_k=0\) by symmetry,
\(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^3=4<\infty\),
but \(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^6=\infty\).
The recursion becomes
\(X_{k+1}^{(\alpha)}=(1-\alpha)X_k^{(\alpha)}+\alpha \xi_k\).
For every \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), \(X_k^{(\alpha)}\) converges in distribution to the stationary AR(1) law
\(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\).
So the existence half is true in this example. However, if
\(R_\alpha:=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 1}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\),
then \(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\) with \(R_\alpha\) independent of \(\xi_0\) and \(\mathbb E R_\alpha=0\), hence by conditional Jensen,
\[
\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}|^6
\ge
\mathbb E\big|\mathbb E[X^{(\alpha)}\mid \xi_0]\big|^6
=
\alpha^6\mathbb E|\xi_0|^6
=
\infty.
\]
Since \(3h=6\), the conjectured moment bound fails for every \(\alpha>0\). Therefore the conjecture is false.

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is not fully well-specified. Assumption 5.1 says \(f\) is of class \(C^h\) but also requires bounded \(f^{(h+1)}\), so formally it is really asking for at least \(C^{h+1}\). Assumption 5.2 is written as \(\mathbb E[\xi_k^3]<\infty\), while the surrounding prose discusses \(\mathbb E|\xi_k|^3<\infty\). More importantly, Assumption 5.2 and Conjecture 5.1 do not explicitly restate the mean-zero condition \(\mathbb E\xi_k=0\) from the earlier i.i.d.-noise Assumption 3.1, even though (3.4) was introduced in that centered-noise setting. Also, because \(h=2\) is explicitly allowed, any correct formulation must pass the quadratic test case above. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

If the conjecture is read literally without a centered-noise assumption, the existence half can fail even more directly: for example, \(f(x)=\log\cosh x\) satisfies Assumption 5.1 with \(h=2\), but with deterministic noise \(\xi_k\equiv 2\) one gets
\(X_{k+1}=X_k+\alpha(2-\tanh X_k)\ge X_k+\alpha\),
so \(X_k\to\infty\) and there is no limiting stationary distribution. So the omission of centering is mathematically consequential, not cosmetic.

Reasoning

The core obstruction is a moment mismatch. In the allowed \(h=2\) case, the conjecture demands a sixth stationary moment from only a third noise moment. For the linear quadratic recursion, the stationary law still contains the current innovation linearly, so any missing sixth moment of the noise is inherited by the stationary distribution.

Here is the verification of the first part. For \(\alpha\in(0,1)\),
\[
X_k=(1-\alpha)^kX_0+\alpha\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_{k-1-j}.
\]
Because \(\mathbb E|\xi_0|<\infty\) and \(\sum_{j\ge 0}\alpha(1-\alpha)^j=1\), the infinite series
\(\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\)
converges absolutely almost surely and in \(L^1\). Thus
\(X_k\Rightarrow X^{(\alpha)}:=\alpha\sum_{j\ge 0}(1-\alpha)^j\xi_j\).
So the counterexample does not attack the existence claim; it attacks the claimed \(3h\)-moment bound.

The second part fails because higher moments are impossible without corresponding innovation moments. Writing
\(X^{(\alpha)}=\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\)
with \(R_\alpha\) independent of \(\xi_0\) and mean zero,
\[
\mathbb E|X^{(\alpha)}|^6
=
\mathbb E\!\left[\mathbb E\!\left(|\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha|^6\mid \xi_0\right)\right]
\ge
\mathbb E\big|\mathbb E[\alpha\xi_0+R_\alpha\mid \xi_0]\big|^6
=
\alpha^6\mathbb E|\xi_0|^6
=
\infty.
\]
So there is no finite \(C_h\), even for a single \(\alpha\), let alone uniformly over \(\alpha\).

A plausible repair would need at least two changes: add centered noise explicitly, and replace the third-moment hypothesis by a moment assumption strong enough to control the desired \(3h\)-th stationary moment, e.g. \(\mathbb E|\xi|^{3h}<\infty\). For a general positive theorem, one would also typically expect a global dissipativity/coercivity condition, not only the local flat-minimum condition in Assumption 5.1. That last sentence is an inference about what a corrected theorem would likely need, guided by nearby dissipativity-based constant-step SGD results rather than a proof of a repaired version. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.07904))

Literature overlap

Best classification: (b) close versions exist in the literature. It cannot be (a) “nearly exact in the literature,” because the exact statement is false. The surrounding theory is already substantial: the present paper proves invariant-distribution existence in the smooth strongly convex case; Chen–Mou–Maguluri study stationary behavior in smooth strongly convex, linear, and contractive settings; Yu–Balasubramanian–Volgushev–Erdogdu analyze constant-step SGD around a unique invariant distribution under dissipativity; Bianchi–Hachem–Schechtman study invariant distributions of constant-step SGD kernels; and Azizian–Iutzeler–Malick–Mertikopoulos give Boltzmann–Gibbs-type long-run descriptions of SGD via large deviations. None of these sources imply the exact Conjecture 5.1 from only Assumptions 5.1–5.2, and the \(h=2\) counterexample explains why. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

I did not find this exact heavy-tail \(h=2\) counterexample written verbatim in the literature. But it is an elementary linear-SA/AR(1) obstruction, so I would not call the disproof itself highly novel; the broader invariant-measure/stability ideas are already well represented in nearby work.

Sources

Primary paper analyzed: Wang, Wang, Narang, Wang, Wang, and Maguluri, arXiv:2602.13960v1, especially Assumptions 5.1–5.2, Conjecture 5.1, recursion (3.4), and Proposition 3.1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13960v1))

Close comparison literature consulted: Chen–Mou–Maguluri on stationary behavior of constant-stepsize SGD/SA; Yu–Balasubramanian–Volgushev–Erdogdu on constant-step SGD under dissipativity; Bianchi–Hachem–Schechtman on invariant distributions for constant-step SGD; Azizian–Iutzeler–Malick–Mertikopoulos on Boltzmann–Gibbs-type long-run distributions of SGD. title: Beyond singular value gaps in randomized subspace approximation
instructions:
You are evaluating a mathematical conjecture from a recent arXiv paper. Work autonomously and use web search whenever it helps. Consult the paper and relevant literature before making claims about priority, novelty, or whether the exact statement is already known.

Your job is to determine whether you can prove the conjecture, refute it by explicit counterexample, identify that the exact result is already essentially in the literature, or conclude that you cannot settle it from the available information.

Requirements:
- Distinguish carefully between the exact conjecture and nearby results.
- Do not claim success unless you can give a mathematically coherent proof sketch or an explicit counterexample with verification.
- If notation or hypotheses are missing, say so clearly and explain how that affects your conclusion.
- If you find the exact result or a close variant in the literature, cite sources and say whether it is (a) nearly exact in the literature, (b) close to existing literature, or (c) relatively novel.
- Cite sources for literature claims.

Return plain text with these section headers exactly:
Verdict
Well-specifiedness
Reasoning
Literature overlap
Sources
output_text:
Verdict

I cannot currently prove or refute Conjecture 5.1 in full generality. I can only confirm the cases already proved in the paper: (i) p = 0,1, where Theorem 4.6 is exactly the conjectured bound, and (ii) the flat-spectrum case Σ1 = a I_k, Σ2 = b I_{N-k}, where Corollary 4.4 gives the same bound with the improved scalar constant. I did not find a rigorous counterexample either. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is reasonably well-specified, but it is not self-contained. To read it precisely, one has to import the hypotheses of Theorem 3.1, the definition of the Frobenius singular value ratio ξ_k from equation (1.2), and the constant C_{N,k,p,δ} from Corollary 4.4. The paper also notes that when σ_k = σ_{k+1}, the SVD partition is non-unique and an arbitrary valid partition is chosen; the conjecture inherits that convention. Also, if k + p ≥ rank(A), then Theorem 3.1 says θ_1 = 0 almost surely, so the conjecture is trivial in that regime. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

Reasoning

The paper’s proof of Theorem 4.6 rewrites the CDF as a mixture
Φ_{Σ,k,p}(θ) = E[P_V(X_θ < V)],
where V has a matrix beta type II density proportional to |V|^((p-1)/2) |I+V|^(-N/2). For p = 0,1, the relevant density is quasiconvex/log-convex enough for the Das Gupta–Anderson symmetrization step used in the proof, and that yields the proved Frobenius-ratio bound. For p > 1, the direct density is no longer quasiconvex because of the |V|^((p-1)/2) factor; the authors explicitly identify this as the obstruction and explain that their current proof instead embeds the problem into a p = 1 beta-II model in a larger dimension, which is exactly what produces the weaker constant in Theorem 4.6. Conjecture 5.1 is the claim that one can somehow avoid that loss. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

I tried to settle the analytically simplest unproved corner case k = 1 and p = N - 2. In that case, if A = diag(σ_1, …, σ_N) and Ω ∈ R^{N×(N-1)}, then Null(Ω^T) is a random line span(u) with u uniform on the sphere, and Null((AΩ)^T) = span(A^{-1}u). Hence
sin^2 θ_1 = (u_1^2 / σ_1^2) / Σ_{j=1}^N (u_j^2 / σ_j^2).
So the conjecture becomes a sharp distributional inequality for a ratio of weighted Dirichlet(1/2, …, 1/2) variables. This makes the problem more concrete, but I still could not prove the needed extremal inequality or derive a counterexample from it.

In the subfamily N = 4, k = 1, p = 2 with two equal tail singular values, the success probability reduces further to a one-dimensional integral against a Beta(1/2, 1/2) law. I did not find a violating parameter choice in that tractable family, but I also could not prove that the flat-spectrum case is extremal there. So this gives some positive evidence, but not a resolution.

What is still missing is a replacement for the p = 0,1 symmetrization step: either a genuine majorization/Schur-convexity statement for X ↦ P_V(X < V) when V is beta-II with p > 1, or a different moment inequality strong enough to recover the scalar constant C_{N,k,p,δ}. I could not establish either. So, from the available information, the mathematically honest conclusion is that the full conjecture remains unsettled.

Literature overlap

I did not find the exact full conjecture already proved elsewhere. The closest overlaps I found are: (1) Saibaba’s randomized-subspace-iteration analysis, which gives canonical-angle bounds in terms of singular-value ratios/gaps, and Wang–Townsend explicitly state that their Theorem 4.8 improves Saibaba’s gap-based theorem; (2) Dong–Martinsson–Nakatsukasa’s later work, which gives practical/asymptotically tight canonical-angle bounds and estimates under moderate oversampling, but not the exact nonasymptotic Frobenius-ratio statement conjectured here; and (3) Wang–Townsend’s own paper, which already proves the conjectured form in the flat-spectrum case and proves the full Frobenius-ratio theorem with a weaker constant for general matrices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.02614))

So for the full conjecture, my assessment is: not (a) nearly exact in the prior literature; yes (b) close versions exist in the literature, especially Theorem 4.6 and Theorem 4.8 of the same paper and the earlier gap-based/asymptotic papers; and the exact full-strength statement appears relatively novel among the sources I checked. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01191?utm_source=openai))

Sources

Wang and Townsend, “Beyond singular value gaps in randomized subspace approximation,” arXiv:2603.01191v1: Theorem 3.1, Corollary 4.4, Theorem 4.6, Theorem 4.8, and Conjecture 5.1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

I cannot currently prove or refute Conjecture 5.1 in full generality. I can only confirm the cases already proved in the paper: (i) p = 0,1, where Theorem 4.6 is exactly the conjectured bound, and (ii) the flat-spectrum case Σ1 = a I_k, Σ2 = b I_{N-k}, where Corollary 4.4 gives the same bound with the improved scalar constant. I did not find a rigorous counterexample either. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is reasonably well-specified, but it is not self-contained. To read it precisely, one has to import the hypotheses of Theorem 3.1, the definition of the Frobenius singular value ratio ξ_k from equation (1.2), and the constant C_{N,k,p,δ} from Corollary 4.4. The paper also notes that when σ_k = σ_{k+1}, the SVD partition is non-unique and an arbitrary valid partition is chosen; the conjecture inherits that convention. Also, if k + p ≥ rank(A), then Theorem 3.1 says θ_1 = 0 almost surely, so the conjecture is trivial in that regime. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

Reasoning

The paper’s proof of Theorem 4.6 rewrites the CDF as a mixture
Φ_{Σ,k,p}(θ) = E[P_V(X_θ < V)],
where V has a matrix beta type II density proportional to |V|^((p-1)/2) |I+V|^(-N/2). For p = 0,1, the relevant density is quasiconvex/log-convex enough for the Das Gupta–Anderson symmetrization step used in the proof, and that yields the proved Frobenius-ratio bound. For p > 1, the direct density is no longer quasiconvex because of the |V|^((p-1)/2) factor; the authors explicitly identify this as the obstruction and explain that their current proof instead embeds the problem into a p = 1 beta-II model in a larger dimension, which is exactly what produces the weaker constant in Theorem 4.6. Conjecture 5.1 is the claim that one can somehow avoid that loss. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.01191v1))

I tried to settle the analytically simplest unproved corner case k = 1 and p = N - 2. In that case, if A = diag(σ_1, …, σ_N) and Ω ∈ R^{N×(N-1)}, then Null(Ω^T) is a random line span(u) with u uniform on the sphere, and Null((AΩ)^T) = span(A^{-1}u). Hence
sin^2 θ_1 = (u_1^2 / σ_1^2) / Σ_{j=1}^N (u_j^2 / σ_j^2).
So the conjecture becomes a sharp distributional inequality for a ratio of weighted Dirichlet(1/2, …, 1/2) variables. This makes the problem more concrete, but I still could not prove the needed extremal inequality or derive a counterexample from it.

In the subfamily N = 4, k = 1, p = 2 with two equal tail singular values, the success probability reduces further to a one-dimensional integral against a Beta(1/2, 1/2) law. I did not find a violating parameter choice in that tractable family, but I also could not prove that the flat-spectrum case is extremal there. So this gives some positive evidence, but not a resolution.

What is still missing is a replacement for the p = 0,1 symmetrization step: either a genuine majorization/Schur-convexity statement for X ↦ P_V(X < V) when V is beta-II with p > 1, or a different moment inequality strong enough to recover the scalar constant C_{N,k,p,δ}. I could not establish either. So, from the available information, the mathematically honest conclusion is that the full conjecture remains unsettled.

Literature overlap

I did not find the exact full conjecture already proved elsewhere. The closest overlaps I found are: (1) Saibaba’s randomized-subspace-iteration analysis, which gives canonical-angle bounds in terms of singular-value ratios/gaps, and Wang–Townsend explicitly state that their Theorem 4.8 improves Saibaba’s gap-based theorem; (2) Dong–Martinsson–Nakatsukasa’s later work, which gives practical/asymptotically tight canonical-angle bounds and estimates under moderate oversampling, but not the exact nonasymptotic Frobenius-ratio statement conjectured here; and (3) Wang–Townsend’s own paper, which already proves the conjectured form in the flat-spectrum case and proves the full Frobenius-ratio theorem with a weaker constant for general matrices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.02614))

So for the full conjecture, my assessment is: not (a) nearly exact in the prior literature; yes (b) close versions exist in the literature, especially Theorem 4.6 and Theorem 4.8 of the same paper and the earlier gap-based/asymptotic papers; and the exact full-strength statement appears relatively novel among the sources I checked. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01191?utm_source=openai))

CONJECTURE_ID: 757
ARXIV_ID: 2603.04218v1
TITLE: Arrow pattern avoidance in permutations: structure and enumeration
AUTHORS: Kassie Archer, Robert P. Laudone
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04218v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2603.04218v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: Arrow-patter-avoidance-in-permutaitons-struccture-enum.tex:802-810

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Arrow patterns were introduced by Berman and Tenner as a generalization of vincular patterns. They observed that arrow patterns have the potential to bridge the divide between a permutation's cycle notation and its one-line notation; in support of this, they used arrow avoidance to enumerate shallow and cyclic shallow permutations. More recently, $321$-avoiding cyclic permutations were recharacterized entirely in terms of arrow avoidance. Motivated by these results, we initiate a systematic study of arrow avoidance. In this paper, we prove structural results about arrow patterns, including defining arrow-Wilf equivalence, and enumerate several arrow avoidance classes. Finally, we consider the avoidance of pairs of arrow patterns, focusing on cases that prohibit fixed points in the underlying permutation.

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
For $n\geq 2,$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $a_n(123, (12; 1 \to 3)) = 2^{n}-n$,
        \item $a_n(321,(12; 1 \to 3)) = F_{2n-1}$, and 
        \item  $a_n(321,(12;1 \to 2)) = M_n$,
    \end{itemize}
     where $F_n$ is the $n$-th Fibonacci number and $M_n$ is the $n$-th Motzkin number.

CONJECTURE_PLAIN_TEXT:
For $n >= 2,$ $a_n(123, (12; 1 to 3)) = 2^ n -n$, $a_n(321,(12; 1 to 3)) = F_ 2n-1 $, and $a_n(321,(12;1 to 2)) = M_n$, where $F_n$ is the $n$-th Fibonacci number and $M_n$ is the $n$-th Motzkin number.

Section: Conjectures and Open Questions
 782:     First, by Lemma \ref{lem: vincArrow}, avoiding $(12;3\to2)$ is equivalent to avoiding $1 \ol{32}$. So, to count $\pi \in \S_n(1 \ol{32},(1;1\to1))$, we first note that $n$ cannot appear to the right of $1$. This is because all entries to the right of $1$ must be increasing to avoid $1 \ol{32}$, so if $n$ appears to the right of $1$ this means $\pi_n = n$, which means $\hat\pi_n = n$, which is not allowed.
 783: 
 784:     Now, consider the permutations with $\pi_k = 1$. As noted above, we must have $\pi_{k+1} < \pi_{k+2} < \cdots < \pi_n$ to avoid a $1 \ol{32}$. Consider the permutation formed by the entries to the left of $1$, $\pi'  = {\rm red}(\pi_1\pi_2\cdots\pi_{k-1})$. Then $\pi'$ must still avoid $1 \ol{32}$ and either avoid fixed points, or have $\pi_{k-1} = k-1$, since $1$ will remove this fixed point in $\pi$. This means there are $a_{k-1} + a_{k-2}$ such permutations. So to construct $\pi$ with $\pi_k = 1$, we first choose the first $k-2$ entries other than $n$, then arrange those entries in $a_{k-1} + a_{k-2}$ ways. The entries after $1$ must be arranged in increasing order. Summing over all allowable positions where $1$ could occur gives:
 785:     \[
 786:     a_n = \sum_{k=2}^{n} \binom{n-2}{k-2}(a_{k-1} + a_{k-2}).
 787:     \]
 788:     Simplifying this sum results in the second stated recursion.
 789: \end{proof}
 790: 
 791: \begin{remark}
 792:     We note that this recursion is identical to the recursion satisfied by the Bell numbers, the difference being that $a_1((12;3\to2),(1;1\to1)) = 0$, whereas $B_1 = 1$. This also implies that if you consider $B_n - a_n((12;3\to2),(1;1\to1))$, the $1\ol{32}$ avoiding permutations that contain a fixed point, they also satisfy the same recursion.
 793: \end{remark}
 794: 
 795: 
 796: \section{Conjectures and Open Questions} \label{sec:conjs}
 797: 
 798: There are many possible future directions of study. In this paper, we've enumerated $\S_n(\alpha)$ for all $\alpha=(\nu;H)\in \A_k$ with $k\in\{1,2,3\}$, $|\nu|\in\{1,2\}$, and $|H|=1$, except for the cases when $\alpha=(12;3\to3)$ or $\alpha=(21;3\to3)$, which remain open. We've also enumerated $\S_n(\alpha,\beta)$ where $\alpha=(12;H)\in\A_2$ or $\A_3$ with $|H|=1$ and $\beta=(1;1\to1).$ There are clearly still many cases that have not yet been considered, but which may have interesting results. In particular, any case where $|\nu|>2$ or where $|H|>2$ are not addressed in this paper.  
 799: 
 800: Additionally, arrow pattern avoidance can be paired with classical pattern avoidance. In these cases, some interesting sequences seem to appear. For example, we make the following conjecture. 
 801: 
 802: \begin{conjecture}
 803:     For $n\geq 2,$
 804:     \begin{itemize}
 805:         \item $a_n(123, (12; 1 \to 3)) = 2^{n}-n$,
 806:         \item $a_n(321,(12; 1 \to 3)) = F_{2n-1}$, and 
 807:         \item  $a_n(321,(12;1 \to 2)) = M_n$,
 808:     \end{itemize}
 809:      where $F_n$ is the $n$-th Fibonacci number and $M_n$ is the $n$-th Motzkin number.
 810: \end{conjecture}
 811: 
 812: 
 813: In Section~\ref{sec:arrows}, we define the notion of arrow-Wilf equivalence. As seen in this paper we have the following arrow patterns in the same arrow-Wilf equivalence class: 
 814: \[
 815: \{(12;1\to2), (12;3\to2), (21;3\to2),(13;1\to2), (13;2\to1),(13;3\to2)\}. 
 816: \]
 817: None of these are explained by Proposition~\ref{prop: rc1}. It would be interesting to have more theorems that explain certain general arrow-Wilf equivalences, if such theorems exist.
 818: 
 819: Finally, as stated in the introduction, certain sets of permutations can be characterized in terms of arrow pattern avoidance, such as shallow permutations or 321-avoiding cyclic permutations. It seems likely that other sets of permutations in which both the one-line and algebraic structure of the permutation are utilized can be described in a similar fashion. Developing techniques for enumerating permutations avoiding arrow permutations could help us develop a better understanding of these sets of permutations, and perhaps enumerate 321-avoiding cyclic permutations, which are currently not well-understood.
 820: 
 821: 
 822: 
 823: \bibliographystyle{amsplain}
 824: \begin{thebibliography}{99}
 825: 
 826: \bibitem{AE14} K. Archer and S. Elizalde, Cyclic permutations realized by signed shifts. \textit{J. Comb.}, 5 (2014), 1--30.
 827: 
 828: \bibitem{Bo21} M. B\'{o}na, Generating functions of permutations with respect to their alternating runs. \textit{S\'eminaire Lotharingien de Combinatoire}, 85 (2021), Article B85b.
 829: 
 830: \bibitem{BC19} M. B\'{o}na and M. Cory, Cyclic permutations avoiding pairs of patterns of length three. \textit{Discrete Math. Theor. Comput. Sci.}, 21(2) (2019), Paper No. 8, 15 pp.
output_text:
Verdict

Partially confirmed. Using the paper’s definitions of arrow containment and the block decomposition coming from the fundamental bijection, I can prove
- \(a_n(321,(12;1\to3))=F_{2n-1}\),
- \(a_n(321,(12;1\to2))=M_n\).

I cannot presently prove or refute
- \(a_n(123,(12;1\to3))=2^n-n\).

So the three-part conjecture is not fully settled here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Well-specifiedness

Inside the paper, the conjecture is well-specified: the paper defines arrow patterns, containment via \(\hat\pi=\theta^{-1}(\pi)\), and \(a_n(\cdots)\) as the size of the corresponding avoidance class. As a standalone statement, though, it is not self-contained without those definitions. I do not see any missing hypotheses beyond \(n\ge 2\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Reasoning

Write a permutation \(\pi\) in blocks
\(B_1B_2\cdots B_m\),
where each block starts at a left-to-right maximum \(M_i\). These are exactly the blocks obtained by removing parentheses from the standard cycle form of \(\hat\pi=\theta^{-1}(\pi)\), so in block \(B_i=M_i\,b_{i,1}\cdots b_{i,r_i}\), the arrows in \(\hat\pi\) are
\(M_i\to b_{i,1}\to\cdots\to b_{i,r_i}\to M_i\).
Also, for a \(321\)-avoiding permutation, the entries that are not left-to-right maxima form a strictly increasing subsequence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

For \(a_n(321,(12;1\to2))\): an occurrence of \((12;1\to2)\) is exactly an adjacent ascent inside one block, because \(x\to y\) with \(x\) before \(y\) means “\(y\) is the next entry after \(x\) in that block,” and the classical part \(12\) means \(x<y\). Hence avoiding \((12;1\to2)\) means every block is decreasing. But in a \(321\)-avoider each block-tail is already increasing, so each block can have at most one nonmaximum entry. Therefore such permutations are in bijection with partial matchings on \([n]\): a block \(Mx\) gives a pair \(\{x,M\}\), and a singleton block gives an unmatched point. Because the nonmaximum entries are globally increasing, these partial matchings are exactly the nonnesting ones. Conversely, any nonnesting partial matching, written in increasing order of right endpoints as blocks \(Mx\) and singleton blocks, produces a \(321\)-avoider with no \((12;1\to2)\). Finally, scanning \(1,2,\dots,n\) and writing \(U\) for a left endpoint, \(D\) for a right endpoint, and \(H\) for a singleton gives a bijection with Motzkin paths of length \(n\) (recover the matching by pairing each \(D\) with the oldest open \(U\)). Hence the class is counted by \(M_n\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

For \(a_n(321,(12;1\to3))\): in a \(321\)-avoider, an internal arrow \(x\to y\) inside a block cannot create an occurrence of \((12;1\to3)\), because the non-left-to-right-maxima are globally increasing, so there is no later value \(z\) with \(x<z<y\). Thus the only possible occurrences come from the last element \(x\) of a block, which maps back to that block’s maximum \(M\). Such an occurrence exists exactly when a later block contains some value \(y\) with \(x<y<M\). Now associate to \(\pi\) the set partition whose blocks are the value-sets of the left-to-right-max blocks. The global increase of the nonmaximum entries is precisely the nonnesting condition. And the condition “some later block contains \(y\) with \(x<y<M\)” is exactly the condition that the final arc \((x,M)\) of one block is crossed by a later block. So this arrow class is in bijection with set partitions that are both nonnesting and noncrossing. Those are one of the standard odd-index-Fibonacci families, so the count is \(F_{2n-1}\). Equivalently, inside \(Av_n(321)\), the arrow condition matches classical avoidance of \(3142\). ([ajc.maths.uq.edu.au](https://ajc.maths.uq.edu.au/pdf/84/ajc_v84_p275.pdf))

For \(a_n(123,(12;1\to3))\), I could not finish the enumeration. What I can show is this: in a \(123\)-avoider, if an occurrence of \((12;1\to3)\) uses an internal arrow \(x\to y\), then adjoining the block maximum \(M\) before \(x\) and a later value \(z\) with \(x<z<y\) gives a classical \(4132\) pattern \(Mxyz\). If the occurrence uses the wrap-around arrow from the last element \(x\) of a block back to its maximum \(M\), then a later block supplies a classical \(3142\) pattern. So the conjectured class lies very close to classical avoidance inside \(Av_n(123)\), but I do not currently have the converse or a derivation of the claimed formula \(2^n-n\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Literature overlap

For the second bullet, I would rate the overlap as (b) close to existing literature, possibly bordering on (a) after translation. My proof identifies the arrow class with noncrossing+nonnesting set partitions, and Gil–Xu–Zhu explicitly list those as an odd-index-Fibonacci family. They also note that several classical pattern-avoidance classes of the form “avoid one 3-pattern and one 4-pattern” are already known to have the same enumeration, and they explicitly prove nearby classes \(Av_n(321,2143)\) and \(Av_n(321,3412)\) have size \(F_{2n-1}\). So the exact arrow wording appears new, but the enumerative content is already very close to the literature. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.15800))

For the third bullet, I would rate the overlap as (b) close to existing literature. The proof lands on nonnesting partial matchings and Motzkin paths, which is classical Motzkin territory. I did not find the exact arrow-pattern statement in the sources I checked, but it clearly fits into the standard crossing/nesting framework for matchings and partitions. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/tran/2007-359-04/S0002-9947-06-04210-3/))

For the first bullet, I cannot classify the overlap confidently. I found a close classical-looking reformulation involving \(3142\) and \(4132\) inside \(Av_n(123)\), but I did not verify an exact prior result or complete the enumeration.

Sources

Archer–Laudone, “Arrow pattern avoidance in permutations: structure and enumeration”: definitions of arrow patterns and avoidance, theorem 3.3 for \((12;1\to2)\), and the conjecture itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Grubb–Rajasekaran, 2022: ballot-pair/noncrossing-partition viewpoint on \(321\)-avoiders, including the fact that in a \(321\)-avoiding permutation the non-left-to-right-maxima are increasing. ([ajc.maths.uq.edu.au](https://ajc.maths.uq.edu.au/pdf/84/ajc_v84_p275.pdf))

Gil–Xu–Zhu, “Odd-indexed Fibonacci numbers via pattern-avoiding permutations”: odd-index Fibonacci interpretations via set partitions with no crossings and no nestings, plus explicit nearby classical classes \(Av_n(321,2143)\) and \(Av_n(321,3412)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.15800))

Chen–Deng–Du–Stanley–Yan, “Crossings and nestings of matchings and partitions”: symmetry background for crossings and nestings in matchings/partitions. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/tran/2007-359-04/S0002-9947-06-04210-3/))

Partially confirmed. Using the paper’s definitions of arrow containment and the block decomposition coming from the fundamental bijection, I can prove
- \(a_n(321,(12;1\to3))=F_{2n-1}\),
- \(a_n(321,(12;1\to2))=M_n\).

I cannot presently prove or refute
- \(a_n(123,(12;1\to3))=2^n-n\).

So the three-part conjecture is not fully settled here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Well-specifiedness

Inside the paper, the conjecture is well-specified: the paper defines arrow patterns, containment via \(\hat\pi=\theta^{-1}(\pi)\), and \(a_n(\cdots)\) as the size of the corresponding avoidance class. As a standalone statement, though, it is not self-contained without those definitions. I do not see any missing hypotheses beyond \(n\ge 2\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Reasoning

Write a permutation \(\pi\) in blocks
\(B_1B_2\cdots B_m\),
where each block starts at a left-to-right maximum \(M_i\). These are exactly the blocks obtained by removing parentheses from the standard cycle form of \(\hat\pi=\theta^{-1}(\pi)\), so in block \(B_i=M_i\,b_{i,1}\cdots b_{i,r_i}\), the arrows in \(\hat\pi\) are
\(M_i\to b_{i,1}\to\cdots\to b_{i,r_i}\to M_i\).
Also, for a \(321\)-avoiding permutation, the entries that are not left-to-right maxima form a strictly increasing subsequence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

For \(a_n(321,(12;1\to2))\): an occurrence of \((12;1\to2)\) is exactly an adjacent ascent inside one block, because \(x\to y\) with \(x\) before \(y\) means “\(y\) is the next entry after \(x\) in that block,” and the classical part \(12\) means \(x<y\). Hence avoiding \((12;1\to2)\) means every block is decreasing. But in a \(321\)-avoider each block-tail is already increasing, so each block can have at most one nonmaximum entry. Therefore such permutations are in bijection with partial matchings on \([n]\): a block \(Mx\) gives a pair \(\{x,M\}\), and a singleton block gives an unmatched point. Because the nonmaximum entries are globally increasing, these partial matchings are exactly the nonnesting ones. Conversely, any nonnesting partial matching, written in increasing order of right endpoints as blocks \(Mx\) and singleton blocks, produces a \(321\)-avoider with no \((12;1\to2)\). Finally, scanning \(1,2,\dots,n\) and writing \(U\) for a left endpoint, \(D\) for a right endpoint, and \(H\) for a singleton gives a bijection with Motzkin paths of length \(n\) (recover the matching by pairing each \(D\) with the oldest open \(U\)). Hence the class is counted by \(M_n\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

For \(a_n(321,(12;1\to3))\): in a \(321\)-avoider, an internal arrow \(x\to y\) inside a block cannot create an occurrence of \((12;1\to3)\), because the non-left-to-right-maxima are globally increasing, so there is no later value \(z\) with \(x<z<y\). Thus the only possible occurrences come from the last element \(x\) of a block, which maps back to that block’s maximum \(M\). Such an occurrence exists exactly when a later block contains some value \(y\) with \(x<y<M\). Now associate to \(\pi\) the set partition whose blocks are the value-sets of the left-to-right-max blocks. The global increase of the nonmaximum entries is precisely the nonnesting condition. And the condition “some later block contains \(y\) with \(x<y<M\)” is exactly the condition that the final arc \((x,M)\) of one block is crossed by a later block. So this arrow class is in bijection with set partitions that are both nonnesting and noncrossing. Those are one of the standard odd-index-Fibonacci families, so the count is \(F_{2n-1}\). Equivalently, inside \(Av_n(321)\), the arrow condition matches classical avoidance of \(3142\). ([ajc.maths.uq.edu.au](https://ajc.maths.uq.edu.au/pdf/84/ajc_v84_p275.pdf))

For \(a_n(123,(12;1\to3))\), I could not finish the enumeration. What I can show is this: in a \(123\)-avoider, if an occurrence of \((12;1\to3)\) uses an internal arrow \(x\to y\), then adjoining the block maximum \(M\) before \(x\) and a later value \(z\) with \(x<z<y\) gives a classical \(4132\) pattern \(Mxyz\). If the occurrence uses the wrap-around arrow from the last element \(x\) of a block back to its maximum \(M\), then a later block supplies a classical \(3142\) pattern. So the conjectured class lies very close to classical avoidance inside \(Av_n(123)\), but I do not currently have the converse or a derivation of the claimed formula \(2^n-n\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Literature overlap

For the second bullet, I would rate the overlap as (b) close to existing literature, possibly bordering on (a) after translation. My proof identifies the arrow class with noncrossing+nonnesting set partitions, and Gil–Xu–Zhu explicitly list those as an odd-index-Fibonacci family. They also note that several classical pattern-avoidance classes of the form “avoid one 3-pattern and one 4-pattern” are already known to have the same enumeration, and they explicitly prove nearby classes \(Av_n(321,2143)\) and \(Av_n(321,3412)\) have size \(F_{2n-1}\). So the exact arrow wording appears new, but the enumerative content is already very close to the literature. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.15800))

For the third bullet, I would rate the overlap as (b) close to existing literature. The proof lands on nonnesting partial matchings and Motzkin paths, which is classical Motzkin territory. I did not find the exact arrow-pattern statement in the sources I checked, but it clearly fits into the standard crossing/nesting framework for matchings and partitions. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/tran/2007-359-04/S0002-9947-06-04210-3/))

For the first bullet, I cannot classify the overlap confidently. I found a close classical-looking reformulation involving \(3142\) and \(4132\) inside \(Av_n(123)\), but I did not verify an exact prior result or complete the enumeration.

Sources

Archer–Laudone, “Arrow pattern avoidance in permutations: structure and enumeration”: definitions of arrow patterns and avoidance, theorem 3.3 for \((12;1\to2)\), and the conjecture itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.04218v1))

Grubb–Rajasekaran, 2022: ballot-pair/noncrossing-partition viewpoint on \(321\)-avoiders, including the fact that in a \(321\)-avoiding permutation the non-left-to-right-maxima are increasing. ([ajc.maths.uq.edu.au](https://ajc.maths.uq.edu.au/pdf/84/ajc_v84_p275.pdf))

Gil–Xu–Zhu, “Odd-indexed Fibonacci numbers via pattern-avoiding permutations”: odd-index Fibonacci interpretations via set partitions with no crossings and no nestings, plus explicit nearby classical classes \(Av_n(321,2143)\) and \(Av_n(321,3412)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.15800))

Chen–Deng–Du–Stanley–Yan, “Crossings and nestings of matchings and partitions”: symmetry background for crossings and nestings in matchings/partitions. CONJECTURE_ID: 670
ARXIV_ID: 2602.04558v1
TITLE: On cyclically covering subspaces of $\mathbb{F}^n_q$
AUTHORS: Yangcheng Li, Pingzhi Yuan, Shuang Li, Yuanpeng Zeng
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04558v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04558v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.04558v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: Cyclically_covering_2026_1_23.tex:617-622

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
For a prime power \( q \) and a positive integer \( n \), a subspace \( U \subseteq \mathbb{F}_q^n \) is called cyclically covering if the union of all its cyclic shifts covers the whole space \( \mathbb{F}_q^n \). Let \( h_q(n) \) denote the maximum possible codimension of such a subspace. This paper focuses on the case \( h_q(n) = 0 \). We provide necessary and sufficient conditions under which \( h_q(n) = 0 \) holds. As an application, we show that \( h_q(\ell^t) = 0 \) whenever \( q \) is a primitive root modulo \( \ell^t \). Moreover, we prove that if \( n \) is odd and \( h_q(n) = 0 \), then also \( h_q(2n) = 0 \). As an example, we show that \( h_3(11) =h_3(16) = 1 \). Furthermore, we investigate the relationship between the coverings of \(\mathbb{F}_{q^m}^n\) and \(\mathbb{F}_q^{mn}\), and obtain several sufficient conditions for \(h_{q^m}(n) = 0\). Specifically, we derive that if \(n = 3\) or \(n = 2^d\) (where \(d\) is a nonnegative integer), then \(h_4(n) = 0\).

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
Let \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) be two \(\sigma\)-invariant subspaces of \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) satisfying \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n} = W_1 \oplus W_2\), where \(\sigma^n = \operatorname{id}\). Then
\begin{equation}\label{eq1}
h_q(n) = h_{\sigma}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}) = \max\{\,h_{\sigma}(W_1),\,h_{\sigma}(W_2)\,\}.
\end{equation}

CONJECTURE_PLAIN_TEXT:
Let \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) be two \(\)-invariant subspaces of \(F_ q^n \) satisfying \(F_ q^n = W_1 W_2\), where \(^n = id\). Then equation h_q(n) = h_ (F_ q^n ) = \,h_ (W_1),\,h_ (W_2)\, . equation

 612: \section{Conclusions and further work}
 613: In this paper, we use isomorphisms of several vector spaces to give some necessary and sufficient conditions for \(h_q(n) = 0\). As an application, we show that \( h_q(\ell^t) = 0 \) whenever \( q \) is a primitive root modulo \( \ell^t \). Moreover, we prove that if \( n \) is odd and \( h_q(n) = 0 \), then \( h_q(2n) = 0 \) also holds. This further yields that \( h_q(2\ell^t) = 0 \) whenever \( q \) is a primitive root modulo \( \ell^t \). As an example, we show that \( h_3(11)=h_3(16)= 1 \). Furthermore, we investigate the relationship between the coverings of \(\mathbb{F}_{q^m}^n\) and \(\mathbb{F}_q^{mn}\), and obtain several sufficient conditions for \(h_{q^m}(n) = 0\). Specifically, we derive that if \(n = 3\) or \(n = 2^d\) (where \(d\) is a nonnegative integer), then \(h_4(n) = 0\).
 614: 
 615: Motivated by theoretical considerations, we further propose an open research conjecture. Following the notation of Theorem \ref{lem4.1}, let \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) be two \(\sigma\)-invariant subspaces of \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\) such that \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n} = W_1 \oplus W_2\), and let \(\overline{U}_1\) be a $\sigma$-covering subspace of \(W_1\). Then \(\overline{U}_1 \oplus W_2\) is a cyclic covering of \(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\). Consequently, we obtain $h_q(n) = h_{\sigma}(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}) \ge \max\{\,h_{\sigma}(W_1),\,h_{\sigma}(W_2)\,\}.$ We propose the following conjecture:

Verdict

Disconfirmed as written. Take q = 2, n = 3, let V = F_{2^3}, let σ = id_V, and choose any direct-sum decomposition V = W1 ⊕ W2, for example W1 = span_{F_2}{1} and W2 any 2-dimensional complement. Since σ is the identity, for every subspace U one has ⋃_{i=0}^{2} σ^i(U) = U, so a σ-covering subspace must be the whole ambient space. Hence h_σ(V) = 0 and likewise h_σ(W1) = h_σ(W2) = 0. But the paper also records h_2(3) = 1. Therefore the asserted equality h_2(3) = h_σ(V) = max{h_σ(W1), h_σ(W2)} fails. So the exact printed conjecture is false. I am not thereby disproving the likely intended repaired version where σ is specifically the Frobenius/cyclic-shift generator; that repaired version I could not settle from the available information. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04558v1))

Well-specifiedness

The conjecture is not well-specified. Earlier in the paper, the identification h_q(n) = h_σ(F_{q^n}) comes from the normal-basis isomorphism with σ taken to be the Frobenius automorphism α ↦ α^q, i.e. the operator corresponding to cyclic shift. But Conjecture 5.1 later assumes only σ^n = id, which also allows many other operators, including σ = id, and under that literal reading the statement is false. There is also a notation problem: Remark 5.1 writes h_q(W1), although the defined invariant-subspace quantity is h_σ(W1). A clean version should explicitly require σ to be the Frobenius/shift generator and should write h_{σ|Wi}(Wi). Also, the printed proof of Theorem 3.1 appears to justify only the weaker construction “if U1 is σ-covering on W1, then U1 ⊕ W2 is σ-covering on the whole space,” not the full biconditional stated there, so the setup around the conjecture is itself somewhat unstable. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04558v1))

Reasoning

The counterexample is immediate from the definition of h_σ. CONJECTURE_ID: 711
ARXIV_ID: 2602.03337v1
TITLE: Vigemers: on the number of $k$-mers sharing the same XOR-based minimizer
AUTHORS: Florian Ingels, Antoine Limasset, Camille Marchet, Mikaël Salson
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03337v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.03337v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: main.tex:414-418

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
In bioinformatics, minimizers have become an inescapable method for handling $k$-mers (words of fixed size $k$) extracted from DNA or RNA sequencing, whether for sampling, storage, querying or partitioning. According to some fixed order on $m$-mers ($m<k$), the minimizer of a $k$-mer is defined as its smallest $m$-mer -- and acts as its fingerprint. Although minimizers are widely used for partitioning purposes, there is almost no theoretical work on the quality of the resulting partitions. For instance, it has been known for decades that the lexicographic order empirically leads to highly unbalanced partitions that are unusable in practice, but it was not until very recently that this observation was theoretically substantiated. The rejection of the lexicographic order has led the community to resort to (pseudo-)random orders using hash functions. In this work, we extend the theoretical results relating to the partitions obtained by the lexicographical order, departing from it to a (exponentially) large family of hash functions, namely where the $m$-mers are XORed against a fixed key. More precisely, provided a key $γ$ and a $m$-mer $w$, we investigate the function that counts how many $k$-mers admit $w$ as their minimizer (i.e. where $w\oplusγ$ is minimal among all $m$-mers of said $k$-mers). This number, denoted by $π_k^γ(w)$, represents the maximum size of the bucket associated with $w$, if all possible $k$-mers were to be seen and partitioned. We adapt the (lexicographical order) method of the literature to our framework and propose combinatorial equations that allow to compute, using dynamic programming, $π_k^γ(w)$ in $O(km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space.

CONJECTURE_LATEX:
Let $g : \Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m$ be such that $g(a_1\cdots a_m) = g_1(a_1)\cdots g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma$. Then, by defining the minimizer $\min_g(x)$ of a \kmer $x$ as its leftmost \mmer $w$ that minimizes $\texttt{rank}(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute
$\pi_k^g(w) = \left\lbrace x\in\Sigma^k : \min_g(x) = w\right\rbrace$
in $O(|\Sigma|\cdot km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.

Section: Conclusion and future work
 394: \end{proof}
 395: 
 396: The Python code for computing $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$, as well as the scripts for reproducing the figures of this paper, is available at \url{https://github.com/fingels/minimizer_counting_function}. In Figure~\ref{fig:numerical results}, we computed $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$ for all possible $w\in\lbrace \ch{A}, \ch{C},\ch{G},\ch{T}\rbrace^m$ ($m=10, k=31$), and for several keys $\gamma$, including the aforementioned orders of the literature: lexicographical, anti-lexicographical \cite[Def.~8.4]{groot2025optimal} and alternating \cite{roberts2004reducing}, plus some random orders. As one can see, the first character of $\gamma$ drives where the peak of the distribution are found, and the subsequent characters drive second-order, so to speak, behaviour of the distribution. If one sorts the buckets by decreasing size (as shown in Appendix~\ref{app:sorting_distributions}), all keys lead to a somewhat identical repartition, allowing us to conclude that the key really does play a role in ``shuffling'' buckets. As a result, no key seems better than another in the sense of producing more balanced distributions, but since the empirical biological data are not uniform, one can still hope to build keys that avoid the \mmer{s} that are too frequent in the data.
 397: 
 398: \begin{figure}[h!]
 399:     \centering
 400:     \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures//vigemin_enumeration_k=31_m=10.pdf}
 401:     \caption{$w\in\lbrace \ch{A}, \ch{C},\ch{G},\ch{T}\rbrace^m\mapsto \pi_k^\gamma(w)$ with $k=31$, $m=10$, for several values of $\gamma$: (left) $\ch{A}\cdots\ch{A}$ is the standard lexicographical order, $\ch{A}\ch{T}\cdots\ch{T}$ is the anti-lexicographical order and $\ch{ATAT}\cdots $ is the alternating order; (right) three random keys starting by \ch{C},\ch{G} and \ch{T}. The solid red horizontal line represents a perfectly balanced partition; the dotted horizontal red line corresponds to empty buckets.}
 402:     \label{fig:numerical results}
 403: \end{figure}
 404: 
 405: Keeping in mind the possible applications mentioned in the introduction --- and ignoring the factor $|\Sigma|$, time complexity in $O(km^2)$ (recall Theorem~\ref{th:complexity}) may still seem quite expensive, especially if we consider that one might want to calculate $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$ for a large number of \mmer{s} $w$ or keys $\gamma$ --- thousands, millions, or even all of them (provided that $m$ is small enough). In particular, it may be more useful to obtain an approximation of the value $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$ faster than to compute the exact value. This option is discussed in Appendix~\ref{app:approximation}.
 406: 
 407: 
 408: 
 409: 
 410: 
 411: \section{Conclusion and future work}
 412: In this work, we extented the method of \cite{ingels2024number} to compute $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$ for any key $\gamma$, whereas \cite{ingels2024number} only allowed $\gamma=\ch{A}\cdots \ch{A}$, i.e. corresponding to the lexicographical order. Therefore, our method allow to access the counting function of $\Sigma^m$ orders on \mmer{s}. We believe that our method could be straightforwardly extended to a broader range of orders on \mmer{s}, that would be of the following form.
 413: 
 414: \begin{conjecture}
 415: Let $g : \Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m$ be such that $g(a_1\cdots a_m) = g_1(a_1)\cdots g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma$. Then, by defining the minimizer $\min_g(x)$ of a \kmer $x$ as its leftmost \mmer $w$ that minimizes $\texttt{rank}(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute
 416: $\pi_k^g(w) = \left\lbrace x\in\Sigma^k : \min_g(x) = w\right\rbrace$
 417: in $O(|\Sigma|\cdot km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.
 418: \end{conjecture}
 419: Indeed, since we fill letter by letter the antemers and postmers, any function $g$ that would also operate letter by letter (such as $g_\gamma(w)=w\oplus\gamma$ in our case) would be compatible with our method. The main difference would be that, since $g_i$ would not be an involution in the general case, we would have to carefully handle cases of equality of the form $g_i(b) = g_i(a_i)$ where $b$ is the letter to determine and $a_i$ the $i$-th letter of $w$. Would this conjecture holds, we would go from covering $|\Sigma|^{m}$ to $|\Sigma|^{m\cdot |\Sigma|}$ orders on \mmer{s} among the $(|\Sigma|^m)!$ possible. To solve more complex cases (e.g., where the $i$-th letter of $g(w)$ depends on $\geq 2$ letters), we conjecture that a completely different approach from the one presented here will have to be developed.
 420: 
 421: Finally, in future work, we plan to exploit the function $\pi_k^\gamma(w)$ for the bioinformatics applications mentioned in the introduction, in particular by studying how to produce a $\gamma$ (equivalently, an order on \mmer{s}) that is suited to a particular dataset and application. In light of the recent idea of using several minimizers/orders on \mmer{s} to improve minimizers-based sampling \cite{ingels2025minimizer}, we wish also to consider using a set of keys $\gamma_1,\dots,\gamma_N$ to determine the best (in a sense to be determined) minimizer among $N$ candidates, e.g. to develop new heuristics for \kmer partitioning.

Verdict

I cannot confirm the conjecture as stated. I can prove a corrected version: if every coordinate map \(g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) is bijective (equivalently, a permutation of \(\Sigma\)), then the same \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\)-time and \(O(km)\)-space dynamic program goes through. For the full statement allowing arbitrary, possibly noninjective \(g_i\), I have neither a proof nor a counterexample to the claimed complexity bound. What I can show is that the extension is not “straightforward” in the sense suggested in v1: noninjective coordinates already break the first edge recurrence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

Well-specifiedness

As written in v1, the conjecture has two specification issues. First, \(\pi_k^g(w)\) is displayed as a set \(\{x\in\Sigma^k:\min_g(x)=w\}\) rather than its cardinality, so absolute-value bars are missing. Second, the paper earlier defines a minimizer order \(O_m\) as an injective map on \(\Sigma^m\), but Conjecture 1 allows arbitrary \(g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma\). If some \(g_i\) is noninjective, then \(w\mapsto \mathrm{rank}(g(w))\) is generally noninjective too, so this is not an order on \(m\)-mers in the paper’s own sense; it is a preorder together with a leftmost tie-break. So the minimizer is still well-defined, but the wording “orders on \(m\)-mers” is literally inaccurate in that generality. If the intended hypothesis was “each \(g_i\) is bijective,” then the statement becomes crisp. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

Reasoning

For the corrected bijective version, let \(w=a_1\cdots a_m\) and assume every \(g_i\) is bijective. Then \(g:\Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m\) is itself a permutation, so \(w\mapsto \mathrm{rank}(g(w))\) really does define a total order on \(m\)-mers. Define the generalized autocorrelation matrix by comparing
\(g_1(a_j)\cdots g_{i-j+1}(a_i)\)
with
\(g_1(a_1)\cdots g_{i-j+1}(a_{i-j+1})\),
and define the specialized alphabets
\(\Sigma_i=\{b\in\Sigma: g_i(b)>g_i(a_i)\}\).
The decomposition used in the paper,
\(x=ywz\) with \(y\) an antemer and \(wz\) a postmer, and the master sum
\(\pi_k(w)=\sum_{\alpha+\beta=k-m}A(\alpha)P_m(\beta+m)\),
use only the leftmost-tie rule and the independence of the left and right parts; they do not depend on any special algebraic identity of XOR. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

In the published XOR proof, the only genuinely special use of “\(\oplus\) is an involution” is to turn equality of transformed letters back into equality of the underlying letters. The clearest example is the \(i=0\) antemer case: the paper rules out \(b_1\oplus c_1=a_1\oplus c_1\) because involutivity forces \(b_1=a_1\). The same inference is valid whenever \(g_1\) is injective. The same phenomenon appears again in the general antemer/postmer steps, where sets of allowed letters have the form \(\Sigma_\ell\cup\{a_\ell\}\); the singleton \(\{a_\ell\}\) is exactly the equality case, and injectivity of \(g_\ell\) is enough to recover it. Therefore Lemma 2, Lemma 4, Proposition 2, Lemmas 6–8, Proposition 3, and then the complexity lemmas transfer verbatim after the substitution \(a\oplus c_i \mapsto g_i(a)\). Since the state space and all preprocessing tables have the same sizes, the \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\) time and \(O(km)\) space bounds are unchanged. So the conjecture is true under the corrected hypothesis “each \(g_i\) is bijective.” ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

For the full noninjective conjecture, I can show the paper’s recurrence scheme no longer works as stated. Take \(\Sigma=\{0,1\}\), \(m=2\), \(w=10\), \(g_1(0)=g_1(1)=0\), and \(g_2\) equal to the identity. Then \(g(w)=00\). The word \(y=0\) is an antemer of length 1, because in \(yw=010\) the only preterminal 2-mer is 01 and \(g(01)=01>00=g(10)\). But the direct analogue of Lemma 2 would use \(\Sigma_1=\{b:g_1(b)>g_1(1)\}=\varnothing\) and would wrongly predict \(A_0(1)=0\). So once transformed equality can happen with letters different from the letters of \(w\), the paper’s state variable “shared prefix length \(i\) with \(w\)” is not sufficient. This is more than a minor bookkeeping issue about equality; it breaks the first edge case of the DP architecture itself. The paper’s own discussion of the conjecture suggests only equality handling as the new issue, and this example shows that is not enough. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

I do not currently have a rigorous lower bound excluding all \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\) algorithms for the noninjective case, so I cannot fully disconfirm the exact conjecture. My conclusion is therefore: corrected bijective version proved; full arbitrary-function version unresolved.

Literature overlap

Among the sources I checked, the exact worst-case bucket-count problem \(\pi_k(\cdot)\) appears explicitly in the lexicographic paper of Ingels–Marchet–Salson from late December 2024 and in the present XOR/key extension from February 3, 2026. I did not find a paper giving the full coordinatewise-function generalization of Conjecture 1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.17492))

For the part I did prove, the right classification is (b) close to existing literature. It is not already stated nearly verbatim, but it is very close: the 2024 paper already treats lexicographical minimizers and even notes that its framework tolerates an arbitrary fixed order on the alphabet itself, while the 2026 paper treats the XOR/key family. My bijective-coordinate theorem is essentially a proof transplant between those two results: the published proof uses injectivity, although the paper phrases the special property as involutivity. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.17492))

For the genuinely noninjective case, I would classify the statement as relatively novel/open based on the literature I checked. Recent minimizer surveys and the introduction of the 2024 counting paper emphasize density, order choice, and applications far more than exact worst-case bucket-size counting under such generalized order surrogates. ([genomebiology.biomedcentral.com](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-024-03414-4))

Sources

Ingels, Limasset, Marchet, Salson, “Vigemers: on the number of k-mers sharing the same XOR-based minimizer,” arXiv:2602.03337v1, submitted February 3, 2026. Used for the conjecture statement, the XOR proof architecture, and the complexity theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03337))

Ingels, Marchet, Salson, “On the number of k-mers admitting a given lexicographical minimizer,” arXiv:2412.17492v2, revised December 24, 2024. Used as the main close antecedent for exact bucket counting in the lexicographic case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.17492))

“When less is more: sketching with minimizers in genomics,” Genome Biology, 2024. Used only for broader minimizer-literature context. ([genomebiology.biomedcentral.com](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-024-03414-4))

I cannot confirm the conjecture as stated. I can prove a corrected version: if every coordinate map \(g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) is bijective (equivalently, a permutation of \(\Sigma\)), then the same \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\)-time and \(O(km)\)-space dynamic program goes through. For the full statement allowing arbitrary, possibly noninjective \(g_i\), I have neither a proof nor a counterexample to the claimed complexity bound. What I can show is that the extension is not “straightforward” in the sense suggested in v1: noninjective coordinates already break the first edge recurrence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

Well-specifiedness

As written in v1, the conjecture has two specification issues. First, \(\pi_k^g(w)\) is displayed as a set \(\{x\in\Sigma^k:\min_g(x)=w\}\) rather than its cardinality, so absolute-value bars are missing. Second, the paper earlier defines a minimizer order \(O_m\) as an injective map on \(\Sigma^m\), but Conjecture 1 allows arbitrary \(g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma\). If some \(g_i\) is noninjective, then \(w\mapsto \mathrm{rank}(g(w))\) is generally noninjective too, so this is not an order on \(m\)-mers in the paper’s own sense; it is a preorder together with a leftmost tie-break. So the minimizer is still well-defined, but the wording “orders on \(m\)-mers” is literally inaccurate in that generality. If the intended hypothesis was “each \(g_i\) is bijective,” then the statement becomes crisp. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

Reasoning

For the corrected bijective version, let \(w=a_1\cdots a_m\) and assume every \(g_i\) is bijective. Then \(g:\Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m\) is itself a permutation, so \(w\mapsto \mathrm{rank}(g(w))\) really does define a total order on \(m\)-mers. Define the generalized autocorrelation matrix by comparing
\(g_1(a_j)\cdots g_{i-j+1}(a_i)\)
with
\(g_1(a_1)\cdots g_{i-j+1}(a_{i-j+1})\),
and define the specialized alphabets
\(\Sigma_i=\{b\in\Sigma: g_i(b)>g_i(a_i)\}\).
The decomposition used in the paper,
\(x=ywz\) with \(y\) an antemer and \(wz\) a postmer, and the master sum
\(\pi_k(w)=\sum_{\alpha+\beta=k-m}A(\alpha)P_m(\beta+m)\),
use only the leftmost-tie rule and the independence of the left and right parts; they do not depend on any special algebraic identity of XOR. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

In the published XOR proof, the only genuinely special use of “\(\oplus\) is an involution” is to turn equality of transformed letters back into equality of the underlying letters. The clearest example is the \(i=0\) antemer case: the paper rules out \(b_1\oplus c_1=a_1\oplus c_1\) because involutivity forces \(b_1=a_1\). The same inference is valid whenever \(g_1\) is injective. The same phenomenon appears again in the general antemer/postmer steps, where sets of allowed letters have the form \(\Sigma_\ell\cup\{a_\ell\}\); the singleton \(\{a_\ell\}\) is exactly the equality case, and injectivity of \(g_\ell\) is enough to recover it. Therefore Lemma 2, Lemma 4, Proposition 2, Lemmas 6–8, Proposition 3, and then the complexity lemmas transfer verbatim after the substitution \(a\oplus c_i \mapsto g_i(a)\). Since the state space and all preprocessing tables have the same sizes, the \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\) time and \(O(km)\) space bounds are unchanged. So the conjecture is true under the corrected hypothesis “each \(g_i\) is bijective.” ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

For the full noninjective conjecture, I can show the paper’s recurrence scheme no longer works as stated. Take \(\Sigma=\{0,1\}\), \(m=2\), \(w=10\), \(g_1(0)=g_1(1)=0\), and \(g_2\) equal to the identity. Then \(g(w)=00\). The word \(y=0\) is an antemer of length 1, because in \(yw=010\) the only preterminal 2-mer is 01 and \(g(01)=01>00=g(10)\). But the direct analogue of Lemma 2 would use \(\Sigma_1=\{b:g_1(b)>g_1(1)\}=\varnothing\) and would wrongly predict \(A_0(1)=0\). So once transformed equality can happen with letters different from the letters of \(w\), the paper’s state variable “shared prefix length \(i\) with \(w\)” is not sufficient. This is more than a minor bookkeeping issue about equality; it breaks the first edge case of the DP architecture itself. The paper’s own discussion of the conjecture suggests only equality handling as the new issue, and this example shows that is not enough. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03337v1))

I do not currently have a rigorous lower bound excluding all \(O(|\Sigma|\cdot k m^2)\) algorithms for the noninjective case, so I cannot fully disconfirm the exact conjecture. My conclusion is therefore: corrected bijective version proved; full arbitrary-function version unresolved.

Literature overlap

Among the sources I checked, the exact worst-case bucket-count problem \(\pi_k(\cdot)\) appears explicitly in the lexicographic paper of Ingels–Marchet–Salson from late December 2024 and in the present XOR/key extension from February 3, 2026. I did not find a paper giving the full coordinatewise-function generalization of Conjecture 1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.17492))

For the part I did prove, the right classification is (b) close to existing literature. It is not already stated nearly verbatim, but it is very close: the 2024 paper already treats lexicographical minimizers and even notes that its framework tolerates an arbitrary fixed order on the alphabet itself, while the 2026 paper treats the XOR/key family. My bijective-coordinate theorem is essentially a proof transplant between those two results: the published proof uses injectivity, although the paper phrases the special property as involutivity. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.17492))

For the genuinely noninjective case, I would classify the statement as relatively novel/open based on the literature I checked. Recent minimizer surveys and the introduction of the 2024 counting paper emphasize density, order choice, and applications far more than exact worst-case bucket-size counting under such generalized order surrogates. ([genomebiology.biomedcentral.com](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-024-03414-4))

CONJECTURE_ID: 678
ARXIV_ID: 2602.04390v1
TITLE: Colored interlacing triangles and Genocchi medians
AUTHORS: Natasha Blitvic, Leonid Petrov
ABSTRACT_URL: http://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04390v1
PDF_URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1
SOURCE_URL: https://arxiv.org/e-print/2602.04390v1
SOURCE_LOCATION: Jan2026_Genocchi.tex:1324-1330

PAPER_ABSTRACT:
Colored interlacing triangles, introduced by Aggarwal-Borodin-Wheeler (2024), provide the combinatorial framework for the Central Limit Theorem for probability measures arising from the Lascoux-Leclerc-Thibon (LLT) polynomials. Colored interlacing triangles depend on two key parameters: the number of colors $n$ and the depth of the triangle $N$. Recent work of Gaetz-Gao (2025) connects these objects to Schubert calculus and resolves the enumeration for $n=3$ and arbitrary depth $N$. However, the enumerative behavior for general $n$ has remained open. In this paper, we analyze the complementary regime: fixed depth $N=2$ and arbitrary number of colors $n$. We prove that in this setting, colored interlacing triangles are in bijection with Dumont derangements, identifying their enumeration with the Genocchi medians. This connects the probabilistic model to a rich hierarchy of classical combinatorial objects. Furthermore, we introduce a $q$-deformation of this enumeration arising naturally from the LLT transition energy. This yields new $q$-analogs of the Genocchi medians. Finally, we present computational results and sampling algorithms for colored interlacing triangles with higher $N$ or $n$, which suggests the limits of combinatorial tractability in the $(N,n)$ parameter space.

Verdict

I cannot currently confirm or disconfirm Conjecture 678. What I can justify is a partial reduction: if the preceding eventual-polynomiality conjecture in the paper (their Conjecture 4.2) is true, then the “k! a_k(n) has integer coefficients” part of Conjecture 4.3 follows formally; the genuinely hard part is the leading-coefficient claim 5^k. I also do not see any counterexample in the data reported in the paper: the authors computed coefficients through q^5 and n up to 15, and those data are consistent with the conjectured formulas and with leading term 5^k/k!. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

Well-specifiedness

As stated, the conjecture is not fully self-contained. The phrase “as polynomials in n” only makes sense after one imports Conjecture 4.2, which says that for each fixed k, the coefficient a_k(n) of q^k in P_n(q) is eventually a polynomial in n of degree k (for n >= n_0(k)). Conjecture 4.3 then speaks about those eventual polynomials, but it does not repeat that qualifier. A cleaner formulation would be: for each fixed k, let p_k(n) be the eventual polynomial with p_k(n)=a_k(n) for all sufficiently large n; then k! p_k(n) lies in Z[n], and its leading coefficient is 5^k. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

Reasoning

Assume Conjecture 4.2, and write p_k(n) for the eventual polynomial equal to a_k(n) for all n >= N_k. Since a_k(n) is an actual coefficient of P_n(q), it is an integer for every integer n in its domain, so p_k(n) takes integer values for all sufficiently large integers. Expand p_k in the Newton basis around some large integer N >= N_k:
p_k(n) = sum_{j=0}^k (Delta^j p_k(N)) * C(n-N,j).
Because p_k(N), p_k(N+1), ..., p_k(N+k) are integers, all forward differences Delta^j p_k(N) are integers. Hence p_k is an integer-valued polynomial. Multiplying by k! gives
k! p_k(n) = sum_{j=0}^k Delta^j p_k(N) * (k!/j!) * (n-N)^{\underline{j}},
and each term has integer coefficients. So, conditional on Conjecture 4.2, the integrality clause in Conjecture 4.3 is essentially automatic. In other words, the real mathematical content of Conjecture 4.3 is the leading coefficient 5^k. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

For that leading coefficient, the paper gives a convincing lower-bound mechanism but not, in my view, a complete proof. Proposition 3.5 proves the k=1 case a_1(n)=5(n-2). Then, immediately after Conjecture 4.3, the authors explain that each internal interface has five canonical local unit-psi defects, and choosing k disjoint internal interfaces gives at least
5^k * binom(n-k,k) = (5^k/k!) n^k + O(n^{k-1})
configurations of total psi equal to k. This proves the lower bound a_k(n) >= (5^k/k!) n^k + O(n^{k-1}). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

What is missing is the matching upper bound. To prove the conjecture, one would need to show that every psi=k configuration is, up to only O(n^{k-1}) exceptions, a union of k disjoint unit defects; equivalently, that there are no other mechanisms contributing an additional n^k family. The paper itself presents this only as “Evidence for Conjecture 4.3,” and I did not find a rigorous argument in the paper or in nearby literature that closes this gap. So I cannot honestly claim a proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

One natural place to look for such a proof is via standard models for the same normalized counting sequence. The paper proves that depth-2 colored interlacing triangles are in bijection with Dumont derangements. Classical literature then gives other models for the normalized median Genocchi numbers, notably Dellac configurations and Hetyei’s biordered-forest / alternation-acyclic-tournament model. Those frameworks look well suited to a finite-cluster analysis of low-psi states, but I did not find an exact theorem there that yields the eventual polynomiality of a_k(n) or the leading coefficient 5^k for this LLT-derived statistic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

Literature overlap

I did not find the exact conjecture already proved in the literature. What does exist is close background: Blitvic–Petrov identify depth-2 colored interlacing triangles with Dumont derangements; Feigin and Bigeni study Dellac configurations and the classical q-analogues attached to normalized median Genocchi numbers; and Hetyei gives a different normalized model via alternation-acyclic tournaments and biordered forests. So there is definitely close surrounding literature. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

But I would not classify the conjecture as “nearly exact in the literature.” The present paper explicitly notes that its q-Genocchi polynomials do not coincide with the previously known q-analogues from Randrianarivony, Han–Zeng/Feigin, or Zeng–Zhou. So the exact fixed-k coefficient conjecture for this specific psi-statistic appears to be relatively novel, even though it sits on top of well-developed nearby models. My classification is therefore: not (a), yes to (b) in the sense of close surrounding machinery, and closer to (c) for the exact statement itself. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

Sources

Blitvic–Petrov, “Colored interlacing triangles and Genocchi medians”: Theorem 2.2 (depth-2 triangles ↔ Dumont derangements), Proposition 3.5 (linear coefficient), and Conjectures 4.2–4.3 with the local-defect heuristic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.04390v1))

Feigin, “Degenerate flag varieties and the median Genocchi numbers,” for Dellac configurations and the classical normalized q-Genocchi setting. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1101.1898))

Bigeni, “Combinatorial study of Dellac configurations and q-extended normalized median Genocchi numbers,” for the Dellac/q-Genocchi framework and related statistics. ([combinatorics.org](https://www.combinatorics.org/ojs/index.php/eljc/article/download/v21i2p32/pdf/))

Hetyei, “Alternation acyclic tournaments,” for the biordered-forest / alternation-acyclic-tournament model of normalized median Genocchi numbers. ([math.charlotte.edu](https://math.charlotte.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/909/2024/03/2017_06.pdf))
